For differentiability at the origin, the **left-derivative must equal the right-derivative**.

**Right-derivative at x = 0:**

$$
\lim_{h \to 0^{+}} \frac{f(0+h) - f(0)}{h} \;=\; \lim_{h \to 0^{+}} \frac{h - 0}{h} \;=\; 1
$$

**Left-derivative at x = 0:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{h \to 0^{-}} \frac{f(0+h) - f(0)}{h} &= \lim_{h \to 0^{-}} \frac{\alpha(e^{h}-1) - 0}{h} \\
&= \alpha \cdot \lim_{h \to 0^{-}} \frac{e^{h}-1}{h}
\end{aligned}
$$

The well-known limit identity gives:

$$
\lim_{h \to 0} \frac{e^{h} - 1}{h} \;=\; 1
$$

Therefore:

$$
\lim_{h \to 0^{-}} \frac{f(0+h) - f(0)}{h} \;=\; \alpha \cdot 1 \;=\; \alpha
$$

**Equating the two one-sided derivatives for differentiability:**

$$
1 \;=\; \alpha
$$

So the function is differentiable at the origin **if and only if α = 1**. ∎

---

## Derivation 4 — Saturation Behaviour as x → −∞

We evaluate the negative-side formula in the limit of very large negative inputs:

$$
\lim_{x \to -\infty} \alpha\big(e^{x} - 1\big) \;=\; \alpha\Big(\lim_{x \to -\infty} e^{x} - \lim_{x \to -\infty} 1\Big)
$$

Because $\lim_{x \to -\infty} e^{x} = 0$:

$$
\lim_{x \to -\infty} \alpha\big(e^{x} - 1\big) \;=\; \alpha(0 - 1) \;=\; -\alpha
$$

**Conclusion:** The output saturates to the finite negative floor **−α**. This bounded negative saturation is a key reason EReLU produces **zero-centered** outputs.

---

## Analytical Matrix — Comparison Across Activation Functions

| Property | ReLU | Leaky ReLU | EReLU (this topic) | Sigmoid |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Positive-side formula** | $x$ | $x$ | $x$ | $\tfrac{1}{1+e^{-x}}$ |
| **Negative-side formula** | $0$ | $0.01\,x$ | $\alpha(e^{x}-1)$ | $\tfrac{1}{1+e^{-x}}$ |
| **Derivative on negative side** | $0$ (dies) | $0.01$ | $\alpha e^{x}$ (non-zero, decays) | $\sigma(x)(1-\sigma(x))$ |
| **Range** | $[0,\infty)$ | $(-\infty,\infty)$ | $(-\alpha,\infty)$ | $(0,1)$ |
| **Zero-centered?** | No | Approx. | Yes (closer to zero) | No |
| **Suffers dying ReLU?** | Yes | No | No | N/A |
| **Smooth at zero?** | No (corner) | No (corner) | Yes if $\alpha=1$ | Yes |
| **Bounded below?** | Yes (0) | No | Yes $(-\alpha)$ | Yes (0) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 1 — Functional Architecture Flow of EReLU Inside a Neuron

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Input Preactivation x] --> nodeB{Decision x greater or equal to 0?}
    nodeB -- Yes path positive --> nodeC[Identity branch f equals x]
    nodeB -- No path negative --> nodeD[Exponential branch f equals alpha times exp x minus 1]
    nodeC --> nodeE[Output Activation a equals x]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Output Activation a equals alpha times exp x minus 1]
    nodeE --> nodeG[Forward to Next Layer]
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH[Backward Gradient Flow during Backprop]
    nodeH --> nodeI{Gradient Branch Selection}
    nodeI -- Positive x --> nodeJ[Gradient equals 1]
    nodeI -- Negative x --> nodeK[Gradient equals alpha times exp x]
```

---

## Diagram 2 — End-to-End Training Flow Highlighting EReLU

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Training Data Batch] --> nodeB[Linear Transformation z equals Wx plus b]
    nodeB --> nodeC[EReLU Activation Function a equals EReLU z]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Next Hidden Layer]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Output Layer Loss Computation]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Backpropagation Chain Rule]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Compute Gradient via EReLU Derivative]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Parameter Update via Optimizer]
    nodeH --> nodeA
```

---

## Diagram 3 — Shape of EReLU Curve (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Region 1 x greater than zero linear identity slope 1] --> nodeB[Passes through origin with continuity]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Region 2 x less than zero exponential curve]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Saturates horizontally toward minus alpha as x decreases]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Output floor at minus alpha ensures bounded negative activation]
```

---

## Diagram 4 — Comparison Block of EReLU vs ReLU vs Leaky ReLU

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Compare Activation Functions] --> nodeB[ReLU Hard zero on negative side]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Leaky ReLU Linear small slope on negative side]
    nodeA --> nodeD[EReLU Exponential curve on negative side with alpha parameter]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Problem Dying ReLU gradient zero]
    nodeC --> nodeF[Advantage Gradient alive but not smooth]
    nodeD --> nodeG[Advantage Smooth curve zero centered bounded gradient]
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read these diagrams:** Each node represents a computational state inside a neuron or training loop. The arrows follow the forward pass first, then the backward gradient flow. Use these diagrams when answering **block-diagram questions (5–7 marks)** in the KTU End-Semester Examination.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Define the EReLU activation function. Write its mathematical form and mention the role of the parameter α.**

**Model Answer:**
EReLU (Exponential Rectified Linear Unit) is a non-linear activation function used in deep neural networks. It is mathematically defined as:

$$
\text{EReLU}(x) =
\begin{cases}
x, & x \ge 0 \\
\alpha(e^{x}-1), & x < 0
\end{cases}
$$

The parameter **α > 0** is a tunable hyper-parameter that controls the magnitude of the negative-side response. When **α = 1**, the function becomes smooth (C¹) at the origin. EReLU removes the dying-ReLU problem by allowing a non-zero gradient on the negative side.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for role of α.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between ReLU and EReLU with respect to gradient flow on the negative side.**

**Model Answer:**
- **ReLU:** On the negative side, the function outputs 0 and its derivative is also **0**. This causes the **dying ReLU problem** — neurons that receive only negative inputs become permanently inactive because no gradient flows backward.
- **EReLU:** On the negative side, the function outputs **α(eˣ − 1)** and its derivative is **α·eˣ**, which is **strictly non-zero** for all finite negative x. Although the gradient decays exponentially for very negative inputs, it never vanishes, so neurons never completely die.

*(Examiner's note: 2 marks for the difference statement, 1 mark for the dying-ReLU reference.)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice)

### Question A `(a)` `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]`

**(a)** Explain the EReLU activation function in detail. Derive its derivative and show that the function is continuous at x = 0. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define EReLU (2 marks):**
The EReLU activation function is given by

$$
\text{EReLU}(x) =
\begin{cases}
x, & x \ge 0 \\
\alpha(e^{x}-1), & x < 0
\end{cases}
$$

For positive inputs it is identical to the identity function; for negative inputs it follows an exponential curve scaled by α.

**Step 2 — Verify continuity at x = 0 (3 marks):**

Right-hand limit:

$$
\lim_{x \to 0^{+}} \text{EReLU}(x) = \lim_{x \to 0^{+}} x = 0
$$

Left-hand limit:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{x \to 0^{-}} \text{EReLU}(x) &= \lim_{x \to 0^{-}} \alpha(e^{x}-1) \\
&= \alpha(e^{0}-1) \\
&= \alpha(1-1) \\
&= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Since both one-sided limits equal 0, the function is **continuous at x = 0**.

**Step 3 — Sketch / qualitative remark (2 marks):**
The curve passes smoothly through the origin (when α = 1) and saturates to the horizontal asymptote **y = −α** as **x → −∞**, while growing unboundedly as **x → +∞**.

---

**(b)** Prove that EReLU is differentiable at x = 0 only when α = 1. State the derivative of EReLU in both regions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the right-derivative (2 marks):**

$$
\lim_{h \to 0^{+}} \frac{\text{EReLU}(0+h)-\text{EReLU}(0)}{h} = \lim_{h \to 0^{+}} \frac{h-0}{h} = 1
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the left-derivative (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{h \to 0^{-}} \frac{\text{EReLU}(0+h)-\text{EReLU}(0)}{h} &= \lim_{h \to 0^{-}} \frac{\alpha(e^{h}-1)-0}{h} \\
&= \alpha \cdot \lim_{h \to 0^{-}} \frac{e^{h}-1}{h} \\
&= \alpha \cdot 1 \quad \text{(standard limit identity)} \\
&= \alpha
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Equate for differentiability (1 mark):**

$$
1 = \alpha \implies \alpha = 1
$$

Hence EReLU is differentiable at x = 0 **if and only if α = 1**.

**Step 4 — State the complete derivative (1 mark):**

$$
f'(x) =
\begin{cases}
1, & x > 0 \\
\alpha\, e^{x}, & x < 0
\end{cases}
$$

---

### Question B `(a)` `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** Compare ReLU, Leaky ReLU, and EReLU activation functions with respect to (i) negative-side formula, (ii) derivative on the negative side, and (iii) range of output. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (in tabular form expected):**

| Aspect | ReLU | Leaky ReLU | EReLU |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) Negative-side formula** | $0$ | $0.01\,x$ | $\alpha(e^{x}-1)$ |
| **(ii) Derivative on negative side** | $0$ | $0.01$ | $\alpha\, e^{x}$ |
| **(iii) Range** | $[0,\infty)$ | $(-\infty,\infty)$ | $(-\alpha,\infty)$ |

**Conclusion (2 marks inside the 7):**
EReLU is the only one of the three whose negative-side output is **bounded below** by **−α**, producing **zero-centered** activations. Its derivative is **non-zero everywhere**, eliminating the dying-ReLU problem.

---

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe how EReLU is incorporated inside a feed-forward neural network during the forward pass. Mention how the gradient is computed during backpropagation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Forward pass block description (3 marks):**
The pre-activation at a neuron is computed as **z = Wᵀx + b**. The activation is then:

$$
a = \text{EReLU}(z) =
\begin{cases}
z, & z \ge 0 \\
\alpha(e^{z}-1), & z < 0
\end{cases}
$$

This activated value **a** is passed as input to the next layer.

**Step 2 — Backward pass gradient computation (3 marks):**
By the chain rule, the local gradient of EReLU is:

$$
\frac{\partial a}{\partial z} =
\begin{cases}
1, & z > 0 \\
\alpha\, e^{z}, & z < 0
\end{cases}
$$

The upstream gradient **∂L/∂a** is multiplied element-wise by this local gradient to produce **∂L/∂z**, which then propagates further back to update **W** and **b** via gradient descent.

**Step 3 — Practical remark (1 mark):**
For numerical stability, implementations usually clamp extremely negative **z** values to avoid **eᵤ** underflow; this matches the saturation behaviour **y → −α** proved analytically.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to state α > 0:** Many students write the formula but do not mention that α is a positive hyper-parameter. (-1 mark)
> 2. **Confusing differentiability with continuity:** Always clarify that EReLU is **always continuous** at 0, but is **differentiable** only when α = 1. (-2 marks)
> 3. **Writing the derivative as α(eˣ − 1):** This is the function value, not the derivative. The derivative on the negative side is **α·eˣ**. (-2 marks)
> 4. **Ignoring saturation limit:** Examiners expect the explicit statement that **lim(x → −∞) EReLU(x) = −α**. (-1 mark)
> 5. **Not mentioning the dying-ReLU fix:** Any 7-mark comparison question without this mention will lose at least **2 marks** because it is the central motivation.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **EReLU = Exponential Rectified Linear Unit.** Formula: **x** for **x ≥ 0**, and **α(eˣ − 1)** for **x < 0**.
- **α > 0** is a tunable hyper-parameter; **α = 1** is the standard default and gives a smooth (C¹) curve.
- **Continuous at x = 0** (both one-sided limits equal 0).
- **Differentiable at x = 0** *only if* **α = 1**.
- **Derivative:** **1** for **x > 0**, **α·eˣ** for **x < 0** — *never zero*, so no dying ReLU.
- **Range:** **(−α, ∞)** — bounded below, unbounded above.
- **Saturation limit:** **lim(x → −∞) EReLU(x) = −α**.
- **Output is approximately zero-centered**, reducing internal covariate shift.
- **Smooth negative-side curve** gives better-conditioned gradients than ReLU and Leaky ReLU.
- **Standard reference value of α = 1.0** in published benchmarks.
- **Used in CNNs, RNNs, Transformers, GANs** wherever vanishing gradients are a concern.
- **Compare-and-contrast** with ReLU, Leaky ReLU, PReLU, ELU, Swish — these are the five functions most often asked alongside EReLU in KTU exams.
- **Numerical tip:** In code frameworks (TensorFlow / PyTorch) extreme negative **z** may underflow **eᵤ**; implementations typically clamp to the saturation floor **−α**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Unsupervised Training of Neural Networks
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST632 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Unsupervised Training of Neural Networks

# Unsupervised Training of Neural Networks — Section 1: Foundations

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Unsupervised Training of Neural Networks** refers to the process of adapting the synaptic weights of a neural architecture using **only unlabelled input data** $\mathcal{D} = \{\mathbf{x}^{(1)}, \mathbf{x}^{(2)}, \dots, \mathbf{x}^{(N)}\}$, where each input $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ has no associated target output. The network must autonomously discover statistical regularities, latent structure, low-dimensional manifolds, clusters, or generative factors hidden within the data distribution $P(\mathbf{x})$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:**  
> Unsupervised learning trains a network $\mathbf{y} = f(\mathbf{x}; \mathbf{W})$ by minimizing an objective $\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{W})$ that depends **only on inputs** — not on any teacher signal. The paradigm includes Hebbian learning, Competitive learning, Self-Organizing Maps (SOM), Autoencoders, and Energy-based models (Boltzmann Machines).

## 1.2 Core Categorization Under KTU Module 2

| Paradigm | Output Type | Typical Use-Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Hebbian / Anti-Hebbian** | Feature extractors (PCs) | Dimensionality reduction |
| **Competitive Learning (Winner-Take-All)** | Cluster indices | Vector Quantization |
| **Self-Organizing Maps (Kohonen)** | 2-D topology map | Visualization, clustering |
| **Autoencoders** | Reconstruction | Non-linear PCA, denoising |
| **Restricted Boltzmann Machines** | Generative samples | Pretraining, collaborative filtering |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "Sorting a Mixed Spice Box"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Chef Without a Recipe Book**  
> Imagine a chef given a basket of unsealed spice jars (unsupervised data). There is **no label** telling her which jar contains cumin or cardamom. She repeatedly **smells, tastes, and groups** similar aromas together — without ever being told the answer. Over time, her neural "taste-buds" (synaptic weights) rewire so that jars with similar profiles cluster together. That is precisely what an unsupervised neural network does: it reshapes its weights so that **statistically similar inputs activate similar internal representations** — purely from the structure of the data itself.

## 1.4 Why Unsupervised Training? (Motivation)

- **Data is cheap, labels are expensive**: Real-world datasets (satellite imagery, genomic sequences, web pages) contain billions of samples but only a tiny fraction are labelled.
- **Feature discovery**: Hand-crafted features miss non-linear structure; unsupervised networks learn **task-agnostic representations** useful for downstream supervised tasks (transfer learning).
- **Generative modelling**: A network trained unsupervised on $P(\mathbf{x})$ can synthesize novel samples — the foundation of modern foundation models.
- **Compression & visualization**: Bottleneck layers of autoencoders and 2-D SOM grids compress high-dimensional data for human inspection.

## 1.5 Key Terminology (KTU Expected Vocabulary)

- **Synaptic weight $w_{ij}$**: Scalar strength of connection from neuron $j$ to neuron $i$.
- **Activation $y_i$**: Scalar output of neuron $i$, often a non-linear function of the weighted sum.
- **Neighbourhood function $h_{ci}$**: A kernel (typically Gaussian) centred on the winning neuron $c$ in a SOM.
- **Reconstruction error**: $\| \mathbf{x} - \hat{\mathbf{x}} \|^{2}$, the canonical loss for autoencoders.
- **Energy function $E(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{h})$**: A scalar score used by Boltzmann machines where low energy = high compatibility.
- **Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC)**: Sampling technique used to estimate statistics of energy-based models.

# Unsupervised Training of Neural Networks — Section 2: Theory & Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hebbian Learning — The Biological Cornerstone

### 2.1.1 Donald Hebb's Postulate (1949)

> *"When an axon of cell A is near enough to excite cell B and repeatedly or persistently takes part in firing it, some growth process or metabolic change takes place in one or both cells such that A's efficiency as one of the cells firing B is increased."*

This is the **biological basis of unsupervised learning**: neurons that fire together wire together.

### 2.1.2 The Canonical Hebbian Update Rule

For a single linear neuron receiving input vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ and producing output $y = \mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}$:

$$\boxed{\Delta w_{i} = \eta \, y \, x_{i}}$$

where $\eta > 0$ is the **learning rate**, $y$ is the post-synaptic activation, and $x_i$ is the pre-synaptic activity.

### 2.1.3 The Weight Explosion Problem

Pure Hebbian learning is **unstable**: since $\Delta w_i$ grows without bound whenever $x_i$ and $y$ are correlated, the weight vector's norm $\| \mathbf{w} \| \to \infty$. Formally:

$$\| \mathbf{w}(t+1) \|^{2} = \| \mathbf{w}(t) + \eta \, y \mathbf{x} \|^{2} \approx \| \mathbf{w}(t) \|^{2} + 2\eta \, y^{2} \, \| \mathbf{w}(t)\| \cos\theta + \eta^{2} y^{2} \| \mathbf{x}\|^{2}$$

The last two terms are always non-negative, so the norm is **monotonically non-decreasing**. This motivates the regularized variants.

## 2.2 Oja's Rule (Single Principal Component)

**Erkki Oja (1982)** introduced a normalized Hebbian update that prevents weight explosion by adding a multiplicative decay proportional to $y^{2}$:

$$\boxed{\Delta w_{i} = \eta \, y \, (x_{i} - y \, w_{i})}$$

Equivalently, in vector form:

$$\Delta \mathbf{w} = \eta \, y \, (\mathbf{x} - y \, \mathbf{w})$$

This can be rewritten as:

$$\Delta \mathbf{w} = \eta \, \big( \mathbf{x}\mathbf{x}^{\top}\mathbf{w} - (\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}\mathbf{x}^{\top}\mathbf{w}) \mathbf{w} \big)$$

which is a **stochastic approximation of the power iteration** for the largest eigenvalue of the input covariance matrix $\mathbf{C} = \mathbb{E}[\mathbf{x}\mathbf{x}^{\top}]$. At convergence, $\mathbf{w}$ points along the **first principal component** of the data, and $\mathbb{E}[y^{2}] = \lambda_{1}$ (the largest eigenvalue).

## 2.3 Sanger's Rule (Multiple Principal Components — Generalized Hebbian Algorithm)

For a network with $m$ output neurons producing $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{W}^{\top}\mathbf{x}$ where $\mathbf{W} \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times m}$, Sanger's rule extracts the **first $m$ principal components in order**:

$$\boxed{\Delta w_{ij} = \eta \, y_{j} \, \left( x_{i} - \sum_{k=1}^{j} w_{ik} \, y_{k} \right)}$$

At convergence, the $j$-th row $\mathbf{w}_{j}$ of $\mathbf{W}$ is the **$j$-th principal eigenvector** of $\mathbf{C}$, and $y_j$ has variance $\lambda_j$.

## 2.4 Competitive Learning & Winner-Take-All (WTA)

In **competitive learning**, output neurons compete: only the **winning neuron $c$** (the one with the largest activation) updates its weights:

$$c = \arg\max_{j} \; \mathbf{w}_{j}^{\top}\mathbf{x}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta \mathbf{w}_{c} = \eta \, (\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{w}_{c}), \qquad \Delta \mathbf{w}_{j} = \mathbf{0} \; \text{for } j \neq c}$$

This drives each weight vector $\mathbf{w}_{j}$ toward the **centroid of the cluster** of inputs it wins. If the neurons are normalized to lie on the unit hypersphere, this becomes **K-means clustering in the limit**.

## 2.5 Kohonen Self-Organizing Map (SOM)

SOM is a 2-D lattice of neurons where each neuron $j$ has a codebook vector $\mathbf{w}_{j} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$. For input $\mathbf{x}$:

1. Find winner: $c = \arg\min_{j} \| \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{w}_{j} \|$.
2. Update winner **and its topological neighbours** using a neighbourhood kernel $h_{cj}(t)$:

$$\boxed{\Delta \mathbf{w}_{j} = \eta(t) \, h_{cj}(t) \, (\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{w}_{j})}$$

The neighbourhood function is typically Gaussian:

$$h_{cj}(t) = \exp\!\left( -\frac{\|\mathbf{r}_{c} - \mathbf{r}_{j}\|^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}(t)} \right)$$

where $\mathbf{r}_{j}$ is the lattice coordinate of neuron $j$. Both $\eta(t)$ and $\sigma(t)$ decay with time, producing a coarse-to-fine ordering.

## 2.6 Autoencoders

An autoencoder is a two-part network — encoder $E: \mathbf{x} \mapsto \mathbf{z}$ and decoder $D: \mathbf{z} \mapsto \hat{\mathbf{x}}$ — trained to minimize:

$$\boxed{\mathcal{L}_{\text{AE}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=1}^{N} \big\| \mathbf{x}^{(n)} - D(E(\mathbf{x}^{(n)})) \big\|^{2}}$$

The bottleneck latent $\mathbf{z} \in \mathbb{R}^{k}$ with $k < d$ forces the network to learn a compressed representation. Non-linear activations (sigmoid, ReLU, tanh) allow autoencoders to learn manifolds that linear PCA cannot capture.

## 2.7 Restricted Boltzmann Machine (RBM)

An RBM is a bipartite Markov random field with visible units $\mathbf{v} \in \{0,1\}^{d}$ and hidden units $\mathbf{h} \in \{0,1\}^{m}$. The energy is:

$$E(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h}) = -\mathbf{b}^{\top}\mathbf{v} - \mathbf{c}^{\top}\mathbf{h} - \mathbf{v}^{\top}\mathbf{W}\mathbf{h}$$

The joint probability is $P(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h}) = \frac{e^{-E(\mathbf{v},\mathbf{h})}}{Z}$ with $Z = \sum_{\mathbf{v},\mathbf{h}} e^{-E}$. The training objective is the **log-likelihood** $\log P(\mathbf{v})$, whose gradient requires samples drawn via **Contrastive Divergence (CD-$k$)**.

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below contains every equation a student must memorize for KTU Module 2 derivations and exam problems.

| # | Concept | Formula | Key Constraint |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pure Hebbian | $\Delta w_i = \eta \, y \, x_i$ | Weights grow unbounded |
| 2 | Oja's rule | $\Delta \mathbf{w} = \eta y (\mathbf{x} - y\mathbf{w})$ | Converges to 1st PC, $\|\mathbf{w}\|=1$ |
| 3 | Sanger (GHA) | $\Delta w_{ij} = \eta y_j \big( x_i - \sum_{k\le j} w_{ik} y_k \big)$ | Extracts first $m$ PCs in order |
| 4 | WTA (Competitive) | $\Delta \mathbf{w}_c = \eta (\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{w}_c)$ | $c = \arg\max_j \mathbf{w}_j^\top \mathbf{x}$ |
| 5 | SOM update | $\Delta \mathbf{w}_j = \eta(t) h_{cj}(t)(\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{w}_j)$ | $h_{cj}$ Gaussian, decays in $t$ |
| 6 | SOM neighbourhood | $h_{cj}(t) = \exp\!\big(-\tfrac{\|\mathbf{r}_c - \mathbf{r}_j\|^2}{2\sigma^2(t)}\big)$ | $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 e^{-t/\tau_\sigma}$ |
| 7 | Autoencoder loss | $\mathcal{L} = \tfrac{1}{N}\sum_n \|\mathbf{x}^{(n)} - D(E(\mathbf{x}^{(n)}))\|^2$ | Bottleneck $k<d$ |
| 8 | RBM Energy | $E(\mathbf{v},\mathbf{h}) = -\mathbf{b}^\top\mathbf{v} - \mathbf{c}^\top\mathbf{h} - \mathbf{v}^\top\mathbf{W}\mathbf{h}$ | Bipartite graph, no $\mathbf{v}$-$\mathbf{v}$ or $\mathbf{h}$-$\mathbf{h}$ edges |
| 9 | RBM conditional | $P(h_j=1\mid\mathbf{v}) = \sigma(c_j + \mathbf{W}_{\cdot j}^\top \mathbf{v})$ | Conditional independence |
| 10 | Power iteration | $\mathbf{w} \leftarrow \frac{\mathbf{C}\mathbf{w}}{\|\mathbf{C}\mathbf{w}\|}$ | Oja's stochastic equivalent |

**Units & Notation Conventions:**
- Weights $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ are real-valued vectors.
- Activations $y \in \mathbb{R}$ for linear, $\in [0,1]$ for sigmoid units.
- Learning rate $\eta$ is dimensionless; typical values $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-1}$.
- Time $t$ in update rules is in **epochs or iterations**, dimensionless index.

# Unsupervised Training of Neural Networks — Section 3: Analytical Derivations

## 3.1 Derivation of the Hebbian Rule from a Correlation Objective

We want the weight vector $\mathbf{w}$ to maximize the correlation between input and output. Define the objective:

$$\mathcal{J}(\mathbf{w}) = \mathbb{E}\!\left[ y^{2} \right] = \mathbb{E}\!\left[ (\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x})^{2} \right] = \mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbb{E}[\mathbf{x}\mathbf{x}^{\top}] \mathbf{w} = \mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{C} \mathbf{w}$$

Apply gradient ascent:

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} \mathcal{J} = 2 \mathbf{C} \mathbf{w} = 2 \, \mathbb{E}[\mathbf{x} \mathbf{x}^{\top}] \mathbf{w}$$

Dropping the factor of 2 and replacing the expectation by an instantaneous sample:

$$\Delta \mathbf{w} = \eta \, \mathbf{x} \, (\mathbf{x}^{\top}\mathbf{w}) = \eta \, y \, \mathbf{x}$$

which recovers the Hebbian rule. **Conversion logic:** The expected outer product $\mathbf{C}$ is replaced by the single-sample estimator $\mathbf{x}\mathbf{x}^{\top}$, yielding the noisy online update.

## 3.2 Oja's Rule Derivation & Stability Proof

### 3.2.1 Mean-Field Differential Equation

Take expected values of the Oja update $\Delta \mathbf{w} = \eta y (\mathbf{x} - y \mathbf{w})$:

$$\frac{d\mathbf{w}}{dt} = \mathbb{E}[y \mathbf{x}] - \mathbb{E}[y^{2}] \mathbf{w}$$

Substitute $y = \mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}$ and $\mathbb{E}[y \mathbf{x}] = \mathbf{C}\mathbf{w}$, $\mathbb{E}[y^{2}] = \mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{C}\mathbf{w}$:

$$\frac{d\mathbf{w}}{dt} = \mathbf{C}\mathbf{w} - (\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{C}\mathbf{w}) \, \mathbf{w}$$

### 3.2.2 Equilibrium Analysis

At equilibrium, $d\mathbf{w}/dt = \mathbf{0}$, so:

$$\mathbf{C}\mathbf{w}^{\star} = (\mathbf{w}^{\star\top}\mathbf{C}\mathbf{w}^{\star}) \, \mathbf{w}^{\star}$$

This is precisely the **eigenvalue equation** $\mathbf{C}\mathbf{w}^{\star} = \lambda \, \mathbf{w}^{\star}$. Therefore $\mathbf{w}^{\star}$ is an eigenvector of $\mathbf{C}$ and $\lambda = \mathbf{w}^{\star\top}\mathbf{C}\mathbf{w}^{\star}$.

### 3.2.3 Norm Preservation

Take the derivative of $\|\mathbf{w}\|^{2} = \mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{w}$:

$$\frac{d\|\mathbf{w}\|^{2}}{dt} = 2 \mathbf{w}^{\top} \frac{d\mathbf{w}}{dt} = 2 \, \mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{C}\mathbf{w} - 2(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{C}\mathbf{w}) \|\mathbf{w}\|^{2}$$

Factoring:

$$\frac{d\|\mathbf{w}\|^{2}}{dt} = 2 \, (\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{C}\mathbf{w}) \, (1 - \|\mathbf{w}\|^{2})$$

This is a logistic differential equation in $s = \|\mathbf{w}\|^{2}$:

$$\frac{ds}{dt} = 2 (\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{C}\mathbf{w}) (1 - s)$$

**Conversion logic:** The logistic form has stable fixed point at $s^{\star}=1$. Hence the weight norm converges to **unity** regardless of initial magnitude, solving the weight-explosion pathology of pure Hebbian learning.

## 3.3 Sanger's Rule Convergence to Ordered PCs

For the Generalized Hebbian Algorithm with $m$ output neurons, consider the weight matrix $\mathbf{W} \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times m}$ with columns $\mathbf{w}_{j}$. Sanger's update in vector form:

$$\Delta \mathbf{w}_{j} = \eta \, y_{j} \, \Big( \mathbf{x} - \sum_{k=1}^{j} y_{k} \, \mathbf{w}_{k} \Big)$$

The subtracted term $\sum_{k=1}^{j} y_{k} \mathbf{w}_{k}$ is the **reconstruction of $\mathbf{x}$ from the first $j$ principal components**. Thus the residual $\mathbf{x} - \sum_{k=1}^{j} y_{k} \mathbf{w}_{k}$ lies in the subspace orthogonal to the first $j$ PCs. Subsequent neurons therefore find the **next** eigenvector in the residual space, guaranteeing ordered extraction.

## 3.4 Competitive Learning as Online K-Means

### 3.4.1 Cost Function

For $m$ codebook vectors $\mathbf{w}_{1},\dots,\mathbf{w}_{m}$, the K-means distortion is:

$$\mathcal{J} = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{m} \mathbb{1}[c(\mathbf{x}^{(n)})=j] \, \| \mathbf{x}^{(n)} - \mathbf{w}_{j} \|^{2}$$

where $c(\mathbf{x}) = \arg\min_{j} \|\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{w}_{j}\|$.

### 3.4.2 Gradient Descent on Winner Only

Taking the gradient w.r.t. $\mathbf{w}_{j}$ and applying it only when $j$ is the winner:

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{w}_{j}} \mathcal{J} = -2 \sum_{n : c(\mathbf{x}^{(n)}) = j} (\mathbf{x}^{(n)} - \mathbf{w}_{j})$$

Stochastic approximation yields:

$$\Delta \mathbf{w}_{j} = \eta \, (\mathbf{x}^{(n)} - \mathbf{w}_{j}) \quad \text{if } j \text{ wins, else } \mathbf{0}$$

**Conversion logic:** This is identical to the WTA rule; competitive learning is online K-means clustering in the limit $\eta \to 0$.

## 3.5 SOM Energy Function and Ordering

The SOM can be interpreted as a discretization of a **Potts-model energy**:

$$\mathcal{E}(t) = \int P(\mathbf{x}) \sum_{j} h_{cj}(t) \, \| \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{w}_{j} \|^{2} \, d\mathbf{x}$$

As $\sigma(t) \to 0$, $h_{cj}(t) \to \delta_{cj}$ and the energy reduces to K-means distortion. The gradual annealing of $\sigma(t)$ is what allows the lattice to first develop **global topological order** before refining locally — this is why pure K-means (no neighbourhood) often yields tangled maps while SOM preserves topology.

## 3.6 Autoencoder Equivalence to PCA (Linear Case)

Consider a linear autoencoder with one hidden layer of $k$ units, $k < d$. Encoder: $\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{W}_{e}^{\top}\mathbf{x}$. Decoder: $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{W}_{d}^{\top}\mathbf{z}$. The optimal weights (minimizing MSE) span the same subspace as the first $k$ principal components. Proof sketch:

1. The total parameter count is $dk + dk = 2dk$. With tied weights $\mathbf{W}_{d} = \mathbf{W}_{e}$, the network learns an orthogonal projection onto the span of $k$ orthonormal basis vectors $\{\mathbf{u}_{1},\dots,\mathbf{u}_{k}\}$.
2. The reconstruction is $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = \sum_{j=1}^{k} (\mathbf{u}_{j}^{\top}\mathbf{x}) \mathbf{u}_{j}$.
3. Minimizing $\mathbb{E}\|\mathbf{x} - \hat{\mathbf{x}}\|^{2}$ over the orthonormal basis is a Rayleigh quotient problem whose solution is the **top-$k$ eigenvectors** of $\mathbf{C}$, identical to PCA.

## 3.7 RBM Log-Likelihood Gradient & Contrastive Divergence

The log-likelihood gradient for a single training example $\mathbf{v}^{(0)}$ is:

$$\frac{\partial \log P(\mathbf{v})}{\partial w_{ij}} = \mathbb{E}_{P(\mathbf{h}\mid\mathbf{v})}[v_i h_j] - \mathbb{E}_{P(\mathbf{v},\mathbf{h})}[v_i h_j]$$

The first term is the **data-dependent expectation** (cheap, since $P(\mathbf{h}\mid\mathbf{v})$ factorizes). The second is the **model expectation**, intractable because it requires summing over all $(\mathbf{v},\mathbf{h})$ configurations. **Contrastive Divergence (CD-$k$)** approximates the second term by running a Gibbs chain for only $k$ steps (typically $k=1$) starting from $\mathbf{v}^{(0)}$:

$$\frac{\partial \log P(\mathbf{v})}{\partial w_{ij}} \approx \langle v_i h_j \rangle_{\text{data}} - \langle v_i h_j \rangle_{\text{recon}}$$

**Conversion logic:** CD-1 replaces the model distribution samples with samples obtained after one positive-phase Gibbs sweep — a biased but computationally cheap estimator that empirically works very well for representation learning.

## 3.8 Summary of Derivations — Analytical Matrix

| Derivation | Source Equation | Result | Engineering Insight |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hebb from correlation | $\mathcal{J} = \mathbb{E}[y^{2}]$ | $\Delta \mathbf{w} = \eta y \mathbf{x}$ | Maximizes variance projection |
| Oja stability | $d\mathbf{w}/dt = \mathbf{C}\mathbf{w} - (\mathbf{w}^\top\mathbf{C}\mathbf{w})\mathbf{w}$ | $\|\mathbf{w}\| \to 1$ | Solves weight explosion |
| Sanger ordering | $\Delta \mathbf{w}_j = \eta y_j (\mathbf{x} - \sum_{k\le j} y_k \mathbf{w}_k)$ | PCs extracted in decreasing $\lambda$ | Generalizes Oja to $m$ outputs |
| WTA = K-means | $\Delta \mathbf{w}_c = \eta(\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{w}_c)$ | Online cluster centroid learning | Foundation of vector quantization |
| Linear AE = PCA | $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = \sum_{j} (\mathbf{u}_j^\top \mathbf{x}) \mathbf{u}_j$ | Subspace = top-$k$ eigenspace | Non-linear AE generalizes this |
| RBM CD-$k$ | $\partial \log P / \partial w_{ij} = \langle v_i h_j\rangle_{\text{data}} - \langle v_i h_j\rangle_{\text{model}}$ | Biased gradient estimate | Pretraining trick for deep nets |

# Unsupervised Training of Neural Networks — Section 5: KTU Question Bank & Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**State and explain the Hebbian learning rule. Why does pure Hebbian learning suffer from weight explosion?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Hebbian learning rule updates the synaptic weight $w_i$ between pre-synaptic neuron $i$ and post-synaptic neuron $j$ according to:

$$\Delta w_i = \eta \, y \, x_i$$

where $\eta$ is the learning rate, $x_i$ is the pre-synaptic input and $y$ is the post-synaptic output. It embodies Hebb's postulate that *"neurons that fire together wire together"*. 

**Why weights explode (1 mark):** Because $\Delta w_i$ has the same sign as $y x_i$ always, repeated application makes the weight grow without bound whenever input and output are positively correlated. Formally, $d\|\mathbf{w}\|^2/dt = 2\eta \, y^2 (\mathbf{w}^\top\mathbf{x}) \geq 0$, so the norm is monotonically non-decreasing.

**Stabilization (1 mark):** Oja's rule fixes this by adding a decay term $-y^2 w_i$, which keeps the weight norm bounded to unity.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between Competitive Learning and Self-Organizing Maps.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Competitive Learning | Self-Organizing Map (SOM) |
|---|---|---|
| Topology | No spatial structure among neurons | Neurons arranged on a 2-D lattice |
| Update | Only the winner updates | Winner + topological neighbours update |
| Neighbourhood kernel | $h_{cj} = \delta_{cj}$ (Kronecker) | $h_{cj}(t) = \exp(-\|\mathbf{r}_c - \mathbf{r}_j\|^2 / 2\sigma^2(t))$, decays with time |
| Result | Cluster centroids (K-means equivalent) | Topology-preserving low-dimensional map |
| Use-case | Vector quantization | Visualization, dimensionality reduction |

(3 marks: 1.5 marks for each side of the comparison with clear differences.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]`

**(a)** Derive Oja's learning rule from the Hebbian rule. Show mathematically that Oja's rule extracts the **first principal component** of the input covariance matrix and that the weight norm converges to unity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **Sanger's Generalized Hebbian Algorithm** with its update equation. How does it differ from Oja's rule, and how does it ensure **ordered extraction** of multiple principal components? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Start with pure Hebbian update:

$$\Delta \mathbf{w} = \eta \, y \, \mathbf{x} = \eta \, (\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}) \, \mathbf{x}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** To prevent weight explosion, Oja added a **multiplicative normalization** term $-\eta y^2 \mathbf{w}$ that drives the norm toward 1:

$$\Delta \mathbf{w} = \eta \, y \, \mathbf{x} - \eta \, y^2 \, \mathbf{w} = \eta \, y \, (\mathbf{x} - y \, \mathbf{w})$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Take expectations to obtain the mean-field ODE. Using $y = \mathbf{w}^\top\mathbf{x}$, $\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{x}\mathbf{x}^\top] = \mathbf{C}$:

$$\frac{d\mathbf{w}}{dt} = \mathbb{E}[y \mathbf{x}] - \mathbb{E}[y^2]\mathbf{w} = \mathbf{C}\mathbf{w} - (\mathbf{w}^\top\mathbf{C}\mathbf{w})\mathbf{w}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** At equilibrium, $d\mathbf{w}/dt = \mathbf{0}$, giving $\mathbf{C}\mathbf{w}^\star = (\mathbf{w}^{\star\top}\mathbf{C}\mathbf{w}^\star)\mathbf{w}^\star$. This is the eigenvalue equation $\mathbf{C}\mathbf{w}^\star = \lambda\mathbf{w}^\star$, so $\mathbf{w}^\star$ is an eigenvector. The associated eigenvalue is $\lambda_1$ (largest) because the objective $\mathbf{w}^\top\mathbf{C}\mathbf{w}$ is maximized under unit norm.

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Norm preservation. Differentiate $\|\mathbf{w}\|^2$:

$$\frac{d\|\mathbf{w}\|^2}{dt} = 2\mathbf{w}^\top \frac{d\mathbf{w}}{dt} = 2\mathbf{w}^\top\mathbf{C}\mathbf{w} - 2(\mathbf{w}^\top\mathbf{C}\mathbf{w})\|\mathbf{w}\|^2 = 2(\mathbf{w}^\top\mathbf{C}\mathbf{w})(1 - \|\mathbf{w}\|^2)$$

This is a logistic ODE with stable fixed point at $\|\mathbf{w}\|=1$. Hence the norm converges to unity regardless of initial conditions, **solving the weight-explosion problem**.

**Step 6 (1 mark):** The output $y = \mathbf{w}^\top\mathbf{x}$ has variance $\mathbb{E}[y^2] = \mathbf{w}^\top\mathbf{C}\mathbf{w} = \lambda_1$, confirming that Oja's rule extracts the **first principal component** with maximal variance.

---

**Part (b) — Sanger's Rule (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Sanger's rule generalizes Oja to $m$ output neurons with weight matrix $\mathbf{W} \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times m}$. The $j$-th output is $y_j = \mathbf{w}_j^\top \mathbf{x}$ and the update is:

$$\Delta w_{ij} = \eta \, y_j \, \left( x_i - \sum_{k=1}^{j} w_{ik} \, y_k \right)$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** In vector form for the $j$-th column:

$$\Delta \mathbf{w}_j = \eta \, y_j \, \left( \mathbf{x} - \sum_{k=1}^{j} y_k \, \mathbf{w}_k \right)$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Ordered extraction mechanism. The bracketed term $\mathbf{x} - \sum_{k=1}^{j} y_k \mathbf{w}_k$ is the **residual** of $\mathbf{x}$ after subtracting its projection onto the first $j$ principal components. This residual lies in the subspace orthogonal to the top-$j$ PCs. Therefore, the $j$-th neuron is forced to find the direction of **maximum remaining variance**, which is exactly $\mathbf{u}_{j+1}$, the next principal eigenvector.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Difference from Oja. Oja has a single output and captures only $\mathbf{u}_1$. Sanger has multiple outputs with the **lateral subtraction term** $\sum_{k=1}^{j} y_k \mathbf{w}_k$, generalizing Oja to extract the first $m$ PCs in **descending order of eigenvalue**.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Convergence. Under the same stochastic approximation arguments as Oja, each $\mathbf{w}_j$ converges to the $j$-th principal eigenvector of $\mathbf{C}$ with unit norm, and $\mathbb{E}[y_j^2] \to \lambda_j$.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**(a)** Describe the architecture and training algorithm of a **Kohonen Self-Organizing Map (SOM)**. Explain the role of the neighbourhood function and its time-decay in producing topology-preserving maps. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the architecture of an **Autoencoder**. Show that a **linear autoencoder with tied weights** is mathematically equivalent to **PCA**. Discuss how a non-linear autoencoder extends beyond PCA. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Kohonen SOM (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Architecture. A SOM consists of $M$ neurons arranged on a regular low-dimensional lattice (typically 2-D rectangular or hexagonal). Each neuron $j$ has an associated codebook (weight) vector $\mathbf{w}_j \in \mathbb{R}^d$ where $d$ is the input dimension. The lattice coordinates $\mathbf{r}_j$ define a topological prior.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Training algorithm. For each input $\mathbf{x}$:
1. Compute activations $d_j = \|\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{w}_j\|$ for all $j$.
2. Find the **winning neuron** $c = \arg\min_j d_j$ (Best Matching Unit, BMU).
3. Update all neurons using:

$$\Delta \mathbf{w}_j = \eta(t) \, h_{cj}(t) \, (\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{w}_j)$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Neighbourhood function $h_{cj}(t)$ is typically Gaussian:

$$h_{cj}(t) = \exp\!\left( -\frac{\|\mathbf{r}_c - \mathbf{r}_j\|^2}{2\sigma^2(t)} \right)$$

where $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 e^{-t/\tau_\sigma}$ decays exponentially. Initially, $\sigma$ is large so many neurons move together — this produces **global topological ordering**. As $\sigma$ shrinks, only the BMU and immediate neighbours update — this produces **fine local refinement**. The same decay applies to $\eta(t)$. This coarse-to-fine annealing is essential for topology preservation.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Result. After training, similar inputs in $\mathbb{R}^d$ activate **nearby neurons on the lattice**, yielding a 2-D visualization where geometric proximity in the map corresponds to similarity in input space.

---

**Part (b) — Autoencoder (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Architecture. An autoencoder is a feed-forward network with two halves. The **encoder** $E_\phi: \mathbb{R}^d \to \mathbb{R}^k$ maps input $\mathbf{x}$ to a latent code $\mathbf{z} = E_\phi(\mathbf{x})$ with $k < d$. The **decoder** $D_\theta: \mathbb{R}^k \to \mathbb{R}^d$ reconstructs $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = D_\theta(\mathbf{z})$. The network is trained to minimize the **reconstruction loss**:

$$\mathcal{L}(\theta, \phi) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=1}^{N} \big\| \mathbf{x}^{(n)} - D_\theta(E_\phi(\mathbf{x}^{(n)})) \big\|^2$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Linear case = PCA. Consider tied weights $\mathbf{W}_d = \mathbf{W}_e = \mathbf{W} \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k}$ and linear activations. Then $\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{W}^\top \mathbf{x}$ and $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{W}\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{W}\mathbf{W}^\top \mathbf{x}$. The MSE loss:

$$\mathcal{L} = \mathbb{E}\big[\|\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{W}\mathbf{W}^\top \mathbf{x}\|^2\big]$$

Minimizing over $\mathbf{W}$ with orthonormal columns gives $\mathbf{W}$ whose columns span the **top-$k$ eigenspace of $\mathbf{C} = \mathbb{E}[\mathbf{x}\mathbf{x}^\top]$** — identical to PCA.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Non-linear extension. Replacing the linear encoder/decoder with non-linear activations (sigmoid, ReLU, tanh) allows the autoencoder to learn a **non-linear manifold** of the data. For example, points lying on a curved Swiss-roll surface in 3-D cannot be linearly projected to 2-D without distortion, but a non-linear autoencoder can "unroll" the manifold. This is impossible for linear PCA.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Variants for KTU awareness. Denoising autoencoders corrupt the input and reconstruct the clean version; sparse autoencoders add an L1 penalty on $\mathbf{z}$; variational autoencoders (VAE) impose a probabilistic prior on $\mathbf{z}$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the decay term in Oja**: Writing $\Delta \mathbf{w} = \eta y \mathbf{x}$ instead of $\Delta \mathbf{w} = \eta y(\mathbf{x} - y\mathbf{w})$ — examiners deduct 2 marks immediately.
> 2. **Mixing Oja and Sanger**: Oja = single PC, **no** subtraction of previous outputs. Sanger = multiple PCs with the **lateral subtraction** $\sum_{k \le j} y_k \mathbf{w}_k$. Conflating them is a 3-mark deduction.
> 3. **SOM neighbourhood kernel formula**: Many students write $h_{cj}(t) = \exp(-\|\mathbf{r}_c - \mathbf{r}_j\|)$ without the squared distance and the factor of 2 in the denominator — examiners check this exact form.
> 4. **Linear AE = PCA only with tied weights AND linear activations**: Forgetting either condition loses the equivalence. Without tied weights, the network has more parameters and the equivalence breaks.
> 5. **RBM bipartiteness**: Stating that visible units are connected to each other is a structural error worth 2 marks — the defining property of RBM is the **absence of intra-layer connections**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this checklist the night before the exam.**

### Must-Know Equations
- [ ] Hebbian: $\Delta w_i = \eta y x_i$
- [ ] Oja: $\Delta \mathbf{w} = \eta y (\mathbf{x} - y \mathbf{w})$
- [ ] Sanger: $\Delta w_{ij} = \eta y_j (x_i - \sum_{k \le j} w_{ik} y_k)$
- [ ] WTA: $\Delta \mathbf{w}_c = \eta (\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{w}_c)$
- [ ] SOM: $\Delta \mathbf{w}_j = \eta(t) h_{cj}(t) (\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{w}_j)$
- [ ] SOM kernel: $h_{cj}(t) = \exp(-\|\mathbf{r}_c - \mathbf{r}_j\|^2 / 2\sigma^2(t))$
- [ ] Autoencoder loss: $\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_n \|\mathbf{x}^{(n)} - D(E(\mathbf{x}^{(n)}))\|^2$
- [ ] RBM energy: $E(\mathbf{v},\mathbf{h}) = -\mathbf{b}^\top\mathbf{v} - \mathbf{c}^\top\mathbf{h} - \mathbf{v}^\top\mathbf{W}\mathbf{h}$

### Must-Know Derivations
- [ ] Hebb from $\mathcal{J} = \mathbb{E}[y^2]$ gradient ascent
- [ ] Oja's norm preservation via logistic ODE $ds/dt = 2(\mathbf{w}^\top\mathbf{C}\mathbf{w})(1-s)$
- [ ] Competitive learning = online K-means
- [ ] Linear autoencoder = PCA (with tied weights and linear activations)

### Must-Know Distinctions
- [ ] Oja vs Sanger: single PC vs ordered multiple PCs
- [ ] Competitive vs SOM: no topology vs topology-preserving
- [ ] Linear AE vs non-linear AE: subspace projection vs manifold learning
- [ ] RBM vs general Boltzmann: bipartite vs fully connected
- [ ] Hebbian vs Anti-Hebbian: positive vs negative weight update

### Must-Know Concepts (One-Liners for 2-mark Questions)
- [ ] **Hebb's postulate**: "Neurons that fire together wire together"
- [ ] **Weight explosion**: Pure Hebbian learning makes $\|\mathbf{w}\| \to \infty$
- [ ] **Oja's stability**: Oja's rule keeps $\|\mathbf{w}\| = 1$
- [ ] **Sanger ordering**: Lateral subtraction $\sum_{k\le j} y_k \mathbf{w}_k$ deflates earlier PCs
- [ ] **BMU**: Best Matching Unit = winning neuron in SOM
- [ ] **Coarse-to-fine annealing**: Decay of $\sigma(t)$ in SOM enables topology preservation
- [ ] **Bottleneck**: Layer of size $k < d$ forces compression in autoencoders
- [ ] **CD-$k$**: Approximation to RBM gradient via $k$-step Gibbs chain
- [ ] **Bipartite graph**: RBM has no intra-layer connections, enabling parallel Gibbs updates

### Frequently Tested Comparisons (Examiner Favourites)
- [ ] Supervised vs Unsupervised (loss function uses labels vs not)
- [ ] PCA vs Autoencoder (linear vs non-linear dimensionality reduction)
- [ ] K-means vs SOM (no topology vs topology)
- [ ] Generative vs Discriminative unsupervised (RBM vs clustering)
- [ ] Oja vs Sanger (single PC vs multiple PCs)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Restricted Boltzmann Machines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST632 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Restricted Boltzmann Machines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Restricted Boltzmann Machine (RBM)** is a two-layer, bipartite, undirected graphical model (a Markov Random Field) consisting of a layer of *visible* stochastic binary units $\mathbf{v} \in \{0,1\}^m$ and a layer of *hidden* stochastic binary units $\mathbf{h} \in \{0,1\}^n$. The "restriction" lies in the graph structure: there are **no intra-layer connections** — a visible unit is connected only to every hidden unit (and vice versa) through symmetric weights $W \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$. This bipartite topology is what enables efficient, exact, parallelizable inference using Gibbs sampling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** RBMs sit at the heart of **Deep Belief Networks (DBNs)** and are the training primitive behind the seminal work of **Hinton & Salakhutdinov (2006)** on greedy layer-wise pre-training of deep networks. Mastering RBMs is essential to understand the historical bridge between shallow generative models and modern deep learning.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Honest Detective"

Imagine a **crime scene with two groups of witnesses**: the *visible* witnesses are the ones the public sees (they describe what they observed), while the *hidden* witnesses are undercover informants (they reveal underlying patterns no one else can see directly). The "restriction" is that no two public witnesses talk to each other, and no two informants talk to each other — they only communicate **through the case file** (the weight matrix $W$). 

When a public witness gives testimony, the entire panel of informants instantly updates their beliefs (because they don't argue amongst themselves — they only take inputs from public testimony). This makes the investigation **fast and parallel**. The detective (the RBM) tries to assign case-file weights so that the public testimonies (visible data) are the *most likely explanation* of the informants' hidden patterns (latent features).

### 1.3 Energy-Based Modeling Intuition

RBMs belong to the family of **Energy-Based Models (EBMs)**. Instead of directly modeling $P(\mathbf{v})$, RBMs assign an *energy scalar* $E(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h})$ to every joint configuration of the system:

- **Low energy** = highly probable configuration (the model "likes" it).
- **High energy** = improbable configuration (the model "disagrees" with it).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why energy?** Boltzmann's original inspiration (statistical mechanics, 1868) showed that complex joint distributions can be elegantly expressed as the exponential of a negative energy divided by temperature. The RBM inherits this elegance.

### 1.4 Visible vs. Hidden Units — The Role of Each Layer

| Unit Type | Symbol | Dimensionality | Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| Visible units | $\mathbf{v}$ | $m$ | Interface with the observed data (pixels, ratings, bag-of-words) |
| Hidden units | $\mathbf{h}$ | $n$ | Learn latent features, dependencies, abstractions |
| Bias (visible) | $\mathbf{a}$ | $m$ | Per-visible-unit activation prior |
| Bias (hidden) | $\mathbf{b}$ | $n$ | Per-hidden-unit activation prior |
| Weights | $W$ | $m \times n$ | Symmetric pairwise interactions $\mathbf{v} \leftrightarrow \mathbf{h}$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Energy Function — Foundational Definition

For a configuration $(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h})$, the RBM assigns the energy:

$$E(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h}) = -\mathbf{a}^{\top}\mathbf{v} - \mathbf{b}^{\top}\mathbf{h} - \mathbf{v}^{\top} W \mathbf{h}$$

Reading each term:
- $-\mathbf{a}^{\top}\mathbf{v}$ : penalty/bias for activating each visible unit.
- $-\mathbf{b}^{\top}\mathbf{h}$ : penalty/bias for activating each hidden unit.
- $-\mathbf{v}^{\top} W \mathbf{h}$ : interaction term — when a visible unit and a hidden unit are both **active**, the weight $W_{ij}$ lowers the energy (favors co-activation).

### 2.2 Joint, Marginal, and Conditional Distributions

Using the Boltzmann distribution (with temperature set to 1):

$$P(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h}) = \frac{1}{Z} \, e^{-E(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h})}$$

The **partition function** $Z$ normalizes over all $2^{m+n}$ possible configurations:

$$Z = \sum_{\mathbf{v}} \sum_{\mathbf{h}} e^{-E(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h})}$$

The **marginal over visible units** (what we actually care about for data modeling) is:

$$P(\mathbf{v}) = \frac{1}{Z} \sum_{\mathbf{h}} e^{-E(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h})} = \frac{e^{-F(\mathbf{v})}}{Z}$$

### 2.3 The Power of the Bipartite Restriction — Factored Conditionals

Because there are **no intra-layer edges**, the hidden units are conditionally independent given $\mathbf{v}$, and vice versa. This factorization is the single most important analytical result in RBM theory:

$$P(\mathbf{h} \mid \mathbf{v}) = \prod_{j=1}^{n} P(h_j \mid \mathbf{v})$$

$$P(\mathbf{v} \mid \mathbf{h}) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} P(v_i \mid \mathbf{h})$$

Each individual conditional is a logistic sigmoid:

$$P(h_j = 1 \mid \mathbf{v}) = \sigma\!\left(b_j + \sum_{i=1}^{m} W_{ij} v_i\right)$$

$$P(v_i = 1 \mid \mathbf{h}) = \sigma\!\left(a_i + \sum_{j=1}^{n} W_{ij} h_j\right)$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** The fact that conditionals factorize as a product of sigmoids is the *definition* of the "restriction." This is what makes inference in RBMs tractable (compared to the general Boltzmann Machine, which is NP-hard).

### 2.4 Free Energy Function

To avoid summing over $2^n$ hidden configurations, we define the **free energy** $F(\mathbf{v})$:

$$F(\mathbf{v}) = -\mathbf{a}^{\top}\mathbf{v} - \sum_{j=1}^{n} \log\!\left(1 + \exp\!\left(b_j + \sum_{i=1}^{m} W_{ij} v_i\right)\right)$$

This collapses the inner sum analytically using the **log-sum-exp trick**, giving us $P(\mathbf{v}) = e^{-F(\mathbf{v})}/Z$ in $O(mn)$ operations instead of $O(2^n)$.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Joint Energy | $E(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h}) = -\mathbf{a}^{\top}\mathbf{v} - \mathbf{b}^{\top}\mathbf{h} - \mathbf{v}^{\top}W\mathbf{h}$ | Foundation of all RBM math |
| 2 | Joint Probability | $P(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h}) = \frac{1}{Z}e^{-E(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h})}$ | Boltzmann distribution |
| 3 | Partition Function | $Z = \sum_{\mathbf{v}}\sum_{\mathbf{h}} e^{-E(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h})}$ | Normalization constant (intractable) |
| 4 | Visible Marginal | $P(\mathbf{v}) = \frac{e^{-F(\mathbf{v})}}{Z}$ | Data likelihood |
| 5 | Free Energy | $F(\mathbf{v}) = -\mathbf{a}^{\top}\mathbf{v} - \sum_j \log(1+e^{b_j + W_{\cdot j}^{\top}\mathbf{v}})$ | Tractable likelihood surrogate |
| 6 | Hidden Conditional | $P(\mathbf{h}\mid\mathbf{v}) = \prod_j \sigma(b_j + W_{\cdot j}^{\top}\mathbf{v})$ | Parallel inference |
| 7 | Visible Conditional | $P(\mathbf{v}\mid\mathbf{h}) = \prod_i \sigma(a_i + W_{i\cdot}\mathbf{h})$ | Reconstruction / generation |
| 8 | CD-k Weight Update | $\Delta W = \varepsilon \left(\langle \mathbf{v}\mathbf{h}^{\top}\rangle_{\text{data}} - \langle \mathbf{v}\mathbf{h}^{\top}\rangle_{\text{model}}\right)$ | Learning rule |
| 9 | Log-Likelihood Gradient | $\frac{\partial \log P(\mathbf{v})}{\partial W_{ij}} = \langle v_i h_j\rangle_{\text{data}} - \langle v_i h_j\rangle_{\text{model}}$ | Theoretical gradient |
| 10 | Sigmoid | $\sigma(x) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-x}}$ | Activation of binary units |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** RBMs are deployed in **collaborative filtering** (Netflix Prize), **feature learning**, **dimensionality reduction**, **topic modeling** (using Replicated Softmax), and as **pre-training modules** for Deep Belief Networks and Deep Boltzmann Machines.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Log-Likelihood Gradient

This derivation is **mandatory for KTU 14-mark questions**. We derive the gradient of the log-likelihood of one training example $\mathbf{v}^{(0)}$ with respect to weight $W_{ij}$.

**Step 1 — Setup the log-likelihood.**

$$\log P(\mathbf{v}) = \log \sum_{\mathbf{h}} e^{-E(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h})} - \log Z$$

Substituting the energy:

$$\log P(\mathbf{v}) = \log \sum_{\mathbf{h}} e^{\mathbf{a}^{\top}\mathbf{v} + \mathbf{b}^{\top}\mathbf{h} + \mathbf{v}^{\top}W\mathbf{h}} - \log Z$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate with respect to $W_{ij}$.**

$$\frac{\partial \log P(\mathbf{v})}{\partial W_{ij}} = \frac{1}{P(\mathbf{v})} \cdot \frac{\partial}{\partial W_{ij}} \sum_{\mathbf{h}} e^{-E(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h})} - \frac{1}{Z}\frac{\partial Z}{\partial W_{ij}}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the quotient rule and the identity $\frac{\partial e^{-E}}{\partial W_{ij}} = v_i h_j \, e^{-E}$.**

The first term becomes the **expectation under the data distribution** $P(\mathbf{h}\mid\mathbf{v})$:

$$\frac{\partial \log P(\mathbf{v})}{\partial W_{ij}} = \sum_{\mathbf{h}} P(\mathbf{h}\mid\mathbf{v}) \, v_i h_j - \sum_{\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h}} P(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h}) \, v_i h_j$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the two expectation terms.**

This gives us the celebrated two-term form of the RBM gradient:

$$\frac{\partial \log P(\mathbf{v})}{\partial W_{ij}} = \langle v_i h_j \rangle_{P(\mathbf{h}\mid\mathbf{v})} - \langle v_i h_j \rangle_{P(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h})}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The first term is the **positive phase** (pushes weights up for features co-active in data). The second term is the **negative phase** (pushes weights down for features the model currently "dreams up"). Learning = balancing these two forces.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Free Energy Function

We start with the marginal:

$$P(\mathbf{v}) = \frac{1}{Z} \sum_{\mathbf{h}} e^{-E(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h})}$$

**Step 1 — Expand the energy in the exponent.**

$$P(\mathbf{v}) = \frac{e^{\mathbf{a}^{\top}\mathbf{v}}}{Z} \sum_{\mathbf{h}} e^{\mathbf{b}^{\top}\mathbf{h} + \mathbf{v}^{\top}W\mathbf{h}}$$

**Step 2 — Factor the sum over hidden units (independence).**

$$\sum_{\mathbf{h}} e^{\mathbf{b}^{\top}\mathbf{h} + \mathbf{v}^{\top}W\mathbf{h}} = \prod_{j=1}^{n} \sum_{h_j \in \{0,1\}} e^{(b_j + W_{\cdot j}^{\top}\mathbf{v}) h_j}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the inner 2-point sum.**

$$\sum_{h_j \in \{0,1\}} e^{(b_j + W_{\cdot j}^{\top}\mathbf{v}) h_j} = 1 + e^{b_j + W_{\cdot j}^{\top}\mathbf{v}}$$

**Step 4 — Take the logarithm to obtain free energy.**

$$F(\mathbf{v}) = -\log P(\mathbf{v}) - \log Z = -\mathbf{a}^{\top}\mathbf{v} - \sum_{j=1}^{n} \log\!\left(1 + e^{b_j + \sum_i W_{ij} v_i}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Final form.**

$$\boxed{F(\mathbf{v}) = -\mathbf{a}^{\top}\mathbf{v} - \sum_{j=1}^{n} \mathrm{softplus}\!\left(b_j + W_{\cdot j}^{\top}\mathbf{v}\right)}$$

where $\mathrm{softplus}(x) = \log(1 + e^x)$.

### 3.3 Contrastive Divergence (CD-k) — The Practical Learning Algorithm

The exact gradient is intractable because $\langle v_i h_j \rangle_{P(\mathbf{v},\mathbf{h})}$ requires samples from the full joint distribution (MCMC over $2^{m+n}$ states). **Hinton (2002)** proposed **Contrastive Divergence (CD-k)** as a fast approximation.

**Algorithm CD-1 (one-step CD):**

1. **Positive phase:** Take a training sample $\mathbf{v}^{(0)}$. Sample $\mathbf{h}^{(0)} \sim P(\mathbf{h}\mid\mathbf{v}^{(0)})$.
2. **Negative phase (reconstruction):** From $\mathbf{h}^{(0)}$, sample $\mathbf{v}^{(1)} \sim P(\mathbf{v}\mid\mathbf{h}^{(0)})$. Then sample $\mathbf{h}^{(1)} \sim P(\mathbf{h}\mid\mathbf{v}^{(1)})$.
3. **Weight update:**

$$\Delta W_{ij} = \varepsilon \left( v_i^{(0)} h_j^{(0)} - v_i^{(1)} h_j^{(1)} \right)$$

$$\Delta a_i = \varepsilon \left( v_i^{(0)} - v_i^{(1)} \right)$$

$$\Delta b_j = \varepsilon \left( h_j^{(0)} - h_j^{(1)} \right)$$

**Why it works:** Even though CD-1 gives a **biased** estimator of the gradient, it is empirically shown to produce good feature detectors. The bias vanishes as $k \to \infty$ (full Gibbs sampling), but in practice $k=1$ is often sufficient.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Conditional Probability Derivations

| Conditional | Derivation Path | Final Closed Form |
|---|---|---|
| $P(h_j = 1 \mid \mathbf{v})$ | Use sigmoid on $b_j + \sum_i W_{ij} v_i$ | $\sigma(b_j + W_{\cdot j}^{\top}\mathbf{v})$ |
| $P(v_i = 1 \mid \mathbf{h})$ | Use sigmoid on $a_i + \sum_j W_{ij} h_j$ | $\sigma(a_i + W_{i\cdot}\mathbf{h})$ |
| $\langle h_j \mid \mathbf{v}\rangle$ | Expectation of Bernoulli | $\sigma(b_j + W_{\cdot j}^{\top}\mathbf{v})$ |
| $\langle v_i h_j \rangle_{\text{data}}$ | Sample mean | $\frac{1}{N}\sum_n v_i^{(n)} h_j^{(n)}$ |

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Computing Conditional Activations

**Given:** $m=2$ visible units, $n=2$ hidden units, $W = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$, $\mathbf{a} = \mathbf{b} = \mathbf{0}$, input $\mathbf{v} = (1, 0)^{\top}$.

**Step 1 — Compute pre-activations for hidden units.**

$$h_1 \text{ pre-activation} = b_1 + W_{11}v_1 + W_{21}v_2 = 0 + 1(1) + (-1)(0) = 1$$

$$h_2 \text{ pre-activation} = b_2 + W_{12}v_1 + W_{22}v_2 = 0 + (-1)(1) + 1(0) = -1$$

**Step 2 — Apply sigmoid.**

$$P(h_1 = 1 \mid \mathbf{v}) = \sigma(1) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-1}} \approx 0.731$$

$$P(h_2 = 1 \mid \mathbf{v}) = \sigma(-1) \approx 0.269$$

**Step 3 — Sample (or take mean) hidden vector.**

If we use **mean-field** (deterministic): $\mathbf{h} = (0.731,\; 0.269)^{\top}$.

**Step 4 — Reconstruct visible pre-activations.**

$$v_1 \text{ pre-activation} = a_1 + W_{11}h_1 + W_{12}h_2 = 0 + 1(0.731) + (-1)(0.269) = 0.462$$

$$v_2 \text{ pre-activation} = a_2 + W_{21}h_1 + W_{22}h_2 = 0 + (-1)(0.731) + 1(0.269) = -0.462$$

**Step 5 — Reconstructed visible probabilities.**

$$P(v_1 = 1 \mid \mathbf{h}) = \sigma(0.462) \approx 0.614$$

$$P(v_2 = 1 \mid \mathbf{h}) = \sigma(-0.462) \approx 0.386$$

**Step 6 — Compare with original $\mathbf{v}=(1,0)$.**

The reconstruction deviates — this **reconstruction error** is precisely the signal that drives the CD-1 weight update. Over many iterations, $W$ adapts so that the reconstruction matches the data on average.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level RBM Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    v1[Visible Unit v1] --- W11[Weight W11]
    v2[Visible Unit v2] --- W12[Weight W12]
    v3[Visible Unit v3] --- W13[Weight W13]
    h1[Hidden Unit h1] --- W11
    h1 --- W21[Weight W21]
    h1 --- W31[Weight W31]
    h2[Hidden Unit h2] --- W12
    h2 --- W22[Weight W22]
    h2 --- W32[Weight W32]
    h3[Hidden Unit h3] --- W13
    h3 --- W23[Weight W23]
    h3 --- W33[Weight W33]
    v1 --- av[Bias a1]
    v2 --- av2[Bias a2]
    v3 --- av3[Bias a3]
    h1 --- bh[Bias b1]
    h2 --- bh2[Bias b2]
    h3 --- bh3[Bias b3]
    style v1 fill:#FFDDC1
    style v2 fill:#FFDDC1
    style v3 fill:#FFDDC1
    style h1 fill:#C1E1FF
    style h2 fill:#C1E1FF
    style h3 fill:#C1E1FF
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Orange nodes = visible layer; Blue nodes = hidden layer. **Note the complete absence of orange-orange and blue-blue edges** — this is the *restriction*. Every orange node connects to every blue node (complete bipartite graph $K_{m,n}$).

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Flow — RBM Inference & Learning Pipeline

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Input Data v0 from Training Set] --> B[Positive Phase: Sample h0 from P h given v0]
    B --> C[Negative Phase Reconstruction: Sample v1 from P v given h0]
    C --> D[Sample h1 from P h given v1]
    D --> E[Compute CD1 Gradient: v0 h0 minus v1 h1]
    E --> F[Update Weights W a b with Learning Rate epsilon]
    F --> G{Convergence Check: Max Iterations or Error Threshold}
    G -->|Not Converged| A
    G -->|Converged| H[Trained RBM Ready for Feature Extraction]
    H --> I[Hidden Representations: Input to DBN or Classifier]
    style A fill:#E8F5E9
    style B fill:#FFF9C4
    style C fill:#FFF9C4
    style D fill:#FFF9C4
    style E fill:#FFCDD2
    style F fill:#FFCDD2
    style H fill:#C5E1A5
    style I fill:#B39DDB
```

### 4.3 Training vs. Inference Mode Comparison

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph INFERENCE[Inference Mode: Clamped Visible]
        V1[Clamp v to Data] --> H1[Compute P h given v in One Parallel Pass]
        H1 --> OUT1[Hidden Activations h as Features]
    end
    subgraph GENERATION[Generation Mode: Free Running]
        H2[Initialize h Randomly] --> V2[Sample v from P v given h]
        V2 --> H3[Sample h from P h given v]
        H3 --> V3[Sample v from P v given h]
        V3 --> H4[Repeat Gibbs Sampling]
    end
    style INFERENCE fill:#E3F2FD
    style GENERATION fill:#FFF3E0
```

### 4.4 Stacking RBMs to Form a Deep Belief Network (DBN)

```mermaid
graph TD
    L1[RBM 1: Visible layer v, Hidden h1] --> L2[RBM 2: Visible layer h1, Hidden h2]
    L2 --> L3[RBM 3: Visible layer h2, Hidden h3]
    L3 --> OUT[Output Layer for Fine Tuning]
    style L1 fill:#FFE0B2
    style L2 fill:#FFCC80
    style L3 fill:#FFB74D
    style OUT fill:#A5D6A7
```

> [!TIP]
> **Greedy Layer-Wise Pre-training:** Each RBM is trained independently using CD-1. Once trained, the hidden activations of RBM 1 are used as "data" to train RBM 2, and so on. After pre-training, the entire stack is **unrolled** and fine-tuned with backpropagation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**  
**State the energy function of a Restricted Boltzmann Machine and explain the role of the weight matrix $W$ in the energy formulation.**

**Model Answer:**

The energy function of a Restricted Boltzmann Machine for a joint state $(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h})$ is:

$$E(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h}) = -\mathbf{a}^{\top}\mathbf{v} - \mathbf{b}^{\top}\mathbf{h} - \mathbf{v}^{\top}W\mathbf{h}$$

**Role of $W$:** The weight matrix $W \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ captures the **symmetric pairwise interaction strength** between visible unit $v_i$ and hidden unit $h_j$. A large positive $W_{ij}$ lowers the energy (increases probability) when both $v_i$ and $h_j$ are simultaneously active, effectively encoding statistical dependencies between observed data and latent features. The symmetric structure ($W = W^{\top}$) reflects the undirected nature of the graphical model.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
**What is meant by the "restriction" in a Restricted Boltzmann Machine? Why is it computationally significant?**

**Model Answer:**

The **"restriction"** in an RBM refers to its **bipartite graph topology**: there are no connections between units within the same layer (no visible-visible or hidden-hidden edges). Only cross-layer connections $\mathbf{v} \leftrightarrow \mathbf{h}$ exist.

**Computational Significance:** This restriction makes the **conditional distributions $P(\mathbf{h}\mid\mathbf{v})$ and $P(\mathbf{v}\mid\mathbf{h})$ factorize** into a product of independent Bernoulli units. This enables:
1. **Parallel Gibbs sampling** — all hidden units can be updated simultaneously in one matrix-vector operation.
2. **Exact inference** for the conditionals using simple sigmoid activations.
3. **Efficient training** via Contrastive Divergence (CD-1), making deep generative pre-training practical.

In contrast, a *general* Boltzmann Machine has intra-layer connections that make inference **NP-hard**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**  
**(a)** Derive the expression for the gradient of the log-likelihood $\frac{\partial \log P(\mathbf{v})}{\partial W_{ij}}$ for an RBM. Clearly show the two expectation terms and explain the role of each in learning. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Contrastive Divergence (CD-1)** algorithm step by step. How does it overcome the intractability of computing the exact gradient? Mention the bias-variance trade-off involved. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of the log-likelihood gradient:**

Starting from the marginal:

$$\log P(\mathbf{v}) = \log \sum_{\mathbf{h}} e^{-E(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h})} - \log Z$$

Differentiating with respect to $W_{ij}$:

$$\frac{\partial \log P(\mathbf{v})}{\partial W_{ij}} = \frac{\sum_{\mathbf{h}} e^{-E(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h})} \cdot v_i h_j}{\sum_{\mathbf{h}} e^{-E(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h})}} - \frac{\sum_{\mathbf{v}', \mathbf{h}} e^{-E(\mathbf{v}', \mathbf{h})} \cdot v'_i h_j}{Z}$$

Recognizing the first term as $P(\mathbf{h}\mid\mathbf{v})$ and the second as $P(\mathbf{v}', \mathbf{h})$:

$$\frac{\partial \log P(\mathbf{v})}{\partial W_{ij}} = \underbrace{\langle v_i h_j \rangle_{P(\mathbf{h}\mid\mathbf{v})}}_{\text{positive phase}} - \underbrace{\langle v_i h_j \rangle_{P(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h})}}_{\text{negative phase}}$$

- **Positive phase:** pushes weights up so that frequently co-active data features are remembered.
- **Negative phase:** pushes weights down so that the model does not assign high probability to hallucinated configurations.

**(b) Contrastive Divergence (CD-1) Algorithm:**

```
Step 1: Take a training sample v^(0)
Step 2: Sample h^(0) ~ P(h | v^(0))   (positive phase)
Step 3: Sample v^(1) ~ P(v | h^(0))   (reconstruction / negative phase start)
Step 4: Sample h^(1) ~ P(h | v^(1))
Step 5: Update:  ΔW = ε [v^(0) h^(0)^T - v^(1) h^(1)^T]
```

**Overcoming intractability:** The exact negative phase requires samples from $P(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h})$, which needs the partition function $Z$ — intractable. CD-1 approximates this by running only **one Gibbs step** from the data distribution, yielding samples $\mathbf{v}^{(1)}, \mathbf{h}^{(1)}$ that approximate the model distribution.

**Bias-Variance Trade-off:** CD-1 produces a **biased** gradient (it uses a model distribution that has not fully mixed), but has **low variance** and is computationally cheap. As $k \to \infty$ (CD-k), the bias vanishes but the cost grows linearly with $k$. Empirically, CD-1 suffices for good feature learning in DBN pre-training.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**  
**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a Restricted Boltzmann Machine. Define the joint probability distribution and partition function. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the conditional probabilities $P(h_j = 1 \mid \mathbf{v})$ and $P(v_i = 1 \mid \mathbf{h})$ for an RBM. Show that they factorize as products of sigmoid functions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) RBM Architecture (refer to Section 4.1 diagram):**

The RBM consists of a **visible layer** $\mathbf{v} = (v_1, \ldots, v_m)$ and a **hidden layer** $\mathbf{h} = (h_1, \ldots, h_n)$, with symmetric weights $W_{ij}$ connecting $v_i$ to $h_j$. No intra-layer connections exist. Bias terms $\mathbf{a}$ (visible) and $\mathbf{b}$ (hidden) are also present.

The joint probability is given by the Boltzmann distribution:

$$P(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h}) = \frac{1}{Z} e^{-E(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h})}$$

where the **partition function** is the sum over all $2^{m+n}$ configurations:

$$Z = \sum_{\mathbf{v} \in \{0,1\}^m} \sum_{\mathbf{h} \in \{0,1\}^n} e^{-E(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h})}$$

$Z$ ensures the distribution is normalized ($\sum_{\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h}} P = 1$), but it is **intractable** to compute exactly for non-trivial sizes.

**(b) Derivation of Conditional Probabilities:**

Starting from Bayes' rule:

$$P(\mathbf{h} \mid \mathbf{v}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h})}{P(\mathbf{v})} = \frac{e^{-E(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h})}}{\sum_{\mathbf{h}'} e^{-E(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h}')}}$$

Substituting the energy and simplifying the denominator (which equals $e^{-F(\mathbf{v})}$):

$$P(\mathbf{h} \mid \mathbf{v}) = \frac{\exp(\mathbf{b}^{\top}\mathbf{h} + \mathbf{v}^{\top}W\mathbf{h})}{\prod_j (1 + e^{b_j + W_{\cdot j}^{\top}\mathbf{v}})} = \prod_{j=1}^{n} \frac{e^{(b_j + W_{\cdot j}^{\top}\mathbf{v}) h_j}}{1 + e^{b_j + W_{\cdot j}^{\top}\mathbf{v}}}$$

Each factor is Bernoulli, giving the individual conditional:

$$P(h_j = 1 \mid \mathbf{v}) = \frac{e^{b_j + W_{\cdot j}^{\top}\mathbf{v}}}{1 + e^{b_j + W_{\cdot j}^{\top}\mathbf{v}}} = \sigma(b_j + W_{\cdot j}^{\top}\mathbf{v})$$

By symmetric argument:

$$P(v_i = 1 \mid \mathbf{h}) = \sigma(a_i + W_{i\cdot}\mathbf{h})$$

The factorization into a product of sigmoids is a **direct consequence of the bipartite restriction**, allowing all hidden units to be sampled in parallel in $O(mn)$ time.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "restriction" justification** in conditional derivations (you must explicitly state "because there are no intra-layer edges, hidden units are conditionally independent given $\mathbf{v}$").
> 2. **Confusing $P(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h})$ with $P(\mathbf{v})$** — the former requires $Z$ and is rarely used directly; the latter is the actual data likelihood.
> 3. **Confusing the positive phase expectation** $P(\mathbf{h}\mid\mathbf{v})$ with the data distribution $P_{\text{data}}(\mathbf{v})$ — they are different concepts.
> 4. **Omitting the bias term** in conditional probability derivations — most students write only the weight term and lose 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Writing CD-1 as a "true" gradient** — it is a biased approximation; examiners look for this nuance in 14-mark answers.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **RBM = bipartite undirected graphical model** with one visible layer $\mathbf{v}$ and one hidden layer $\mathbf{h}$, no intra-layer edges.
- ✅ **Energy function:** $E(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h}) = -\mathbf{a}^{\top}\mathbf{v} - \mathbf{b}^{\top}\mathbf{h} - \mathbf{v}^{\top}W\mathbf{h}$.
- ✅ **Boltzmann distribution:** $P(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h}) = \frac{1}{Z}e^{-E(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h})}$.
- ✅ **Partition function $Z$ is intractable** — sum over $2^{m+n}$ configurations.
- ✅ **Free energy** $F(\mathbf{v})$ marginalizes the hidden layer analytically using softplus.
- ✅ **Conditional independence** (the "restriction" payoff) → conditionals factorize as products of sigmoids.
- ✅ **Hidden conditional:** $P(h_j=1 \mid \mathbf{v}) = \sigma(b_j + W_{\cdot j}^{\top}\mathbf{v})$.
- ✅ **Visible conditional:** $P(v_i=1 \mid \mathbf{h}) = \sigma(a_i + W_{i\cdot}\mathbf{h})$.
- ✅ **Log-likelihood gradient** = positive phase expectation − negative phase expectation.
- ✅ **Positive phase** uses $P(\mathbf{h}\mid\mathbf{v})$ — depends on data.
- ✅ **Negative phase** uses $P(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h})$ — model distribution (intractable).
- ✅ **Contrastive Divergence (CD-1)** uses one Gibbs step to approximate the negative phase.
- ✅ **CD-1 is biased but low variance** — empirically works well for DBN pre-training.
- ✅ **CD-k** generalizes to $k$ Gibbs steps; bias $\to 0$ as $k \to \infty$.
- ✅ **Stacked RBMs** form Deep Belief Networks (DBNs) via greedy layer-wise training.
- ✅ **Sigmoid function:** $\sigma(x) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-x}}$ — activation of every binary unit.
- ✅ **Symmetric weights:** $W$ is the same matrix used for both $P(\mathbf{h}\mid\mathbf{v})$ and $P(\mathbf{v}\mid\mathbf{h})$.
- ✅ **Biases** $\mathbf{a}$ and $\mathbf{b}$ are per-unit thresholds controlling base activation rates.
- ✅ **Applications:** collaborative filtering (Netflix), dimensionality reduction, pre-training deep nets, topic modeling.
- ✅ **Key historical reference:** Hinton's 2006 Science paper popularized RBM-based DBN pre-training.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Autoencoders
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST632 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Autoencoders

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Autoencoder (AE)** is a specialized class of **unsupervised feedforward neural networks** designed and trained to reconstruct its own input data at the output layer. Formally, it is a deterministic mapping parameterized by two functions — an **encoder** $f_{\theta}: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Z}$ and a **decoder** $g_{\phi}: \mathcal{Z} \rightarrow \mathcal{X}$ — such that the composite reconstruction $g_{\phi}(f_{\theta}(x))$ minimizes a reconstruction loss $\mathcal{L}(x, g_{\phi}(f_{\theta}(x)))$ over the dataset.

The intermediate bottleneck layer $\mathcal{Z} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ where $d \ll \dim(\mathcal{X})$ is called the **latent space** or **bottleneck code**. This constraint forces the network to learn a **compressed, lower-dimensional manifold representation** of the input — making autoencoders the canonical architecture for **representation learning**, **dimensionality reduction**, **anomaly detection**, and **generative pre-training**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Autoencoders are explicitly listed under Module 2 of PECST632 alongside Boltzmann Machines and RBMs. The examiner expects coverage of: (i) vanilla undercomplete AE, (ii) sparse AE, (iii) denoising AE, and (iv) variational AE (VAE) at minimum.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Engineering Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Diplomat's Courier" System:**
> Imagine a diplomat carrying a bulky classified suitcase through a security checkpoint. The checkpoint has a strict size limit, so the diplomat is forced to **compress** the suitcase contents into a tiny secure pouch (the **latent code**). On the other side, an authorized receiver uses a **decompression protocol** (the **decoder**) to restore the suitcase to its original contents. The reconstruction is not perfect pixel-by-pixel, but it must preserve the **essential information** required for the mission. The autoencoder operates identically: the encoder compresses, the decoder reconstructs, and the network learns to preserve *semantic essence* rather than raw bits.

A more relatable engineering analogy is **JPEG image compression**: the encoder mimics the Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT) + quantization stage that throws away imperceptible high-frequency details, and the decoder mimics the inverse DCT reconstruction.

## 1.3 Three Structural Components

1. **Encoder** $f_{\theta}(x)$ — Maps high-dimensional input $x \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ to a low-dimensional latent code $z \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$.
2. **Latent (Code) Layer** $z$ — The compressed information bottleneck. If $d < n$, the AE is **undercomplete**; if $d > n$, it is **overcomplete** (requiring regularization to prevent identity learning).
3. **Decoder** $g_{\phi}(z)$ — Maps latent code back to reconstruction $\hat{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$.

The KTU 2024 scheme **Course Outcome (CO2)** for Module 2 specifically requires students to *analyze the architectural role of latent representations in unsupervised deep models*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

The autoencoder forward pass proceeds in three deterministic stages:

- **Stage 1 — Encoding:** The input vector $x \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is projected through one or more hidden layers using affine transformations followed by nonlinear activations. The output is the latent code $z = f_{\theta}(x) = \sigma(W x + b)$ where $\sigma$ is typically $\text{ReLU}$ or $\tanh$, $W$ is the encoder weight matrix, and $b$ is the bias vector.

- **Stage 2 — Bottleneck Constraint:** The dimensionality of $z$ is deliberately constrained. This **information-theoretic bottleneck** is the *core inductive bias* of the autoencoder — without it, the network trivially learns the identity function $g_{\phi}(f_{\theta}(x)) = x$ and provides no useful representation.

- **Stage 3 — Decoding:** The latent code is expanded back to the original dimensionality via $\hat{x} = g_{\phi}(z) = \sigma'(W' z + b')$. The decoder is often architecturally *mirrored* to the encoder (so-called *tied weights* when $W' = W^{T}$).

- **Stage 4 — Loss Computation:** A reconstruction loss $\mathcal{L}(x, \hat{x})$ is computed between the original input and its reconstruction. Gradients are backpropagated through the entire composite network $g_{\phi} \circ f_{\theta}$.

> [!TIP]
> **Why "Why" Matters in KTU Exams:** Examiners frequently award marks for explaining *why* a bottleneck is necessary. A model answer must state that without the bottleneck, the AE degenerates into a trivial identity function and learns no useful compressed features.

## 2.2 Loss Functions — The Mathematical Heart

The choice of loss function determines what the autoencoder considers "similar" to the original input:

- For **real-valued data** (e.g., normalized images in $[0,1]$), the standard **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** or **Binary Cross-Entropy (BCE)** are used.
- For **sparse binary or one-hot encoded data**, **Kullback–Leibler (KL) divergence** may be used.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Equation | Description | Engineering Use |
|----------|-------------|-----------------|
| $z = f_{\theta}(x) = \sigma(W x + b)$ | Encoder forward map | Feature extraction / compression |
| $\hat{x} = g_{\phi}(z) = \sigma'(W' z + b')$ | Decoder forward map | Signal reconstruction |
| $\mathcal{L}_{\text{MSE}}(x, \hat{x}) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \hat{x}_i)^{2}$ | Pixel-wise reconstruction loss | Real-valued regression-style inputs |
| $\mathcal{L}_{\text{BCE}}(x, \hat{x}) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left[x_i \log \hat{x}_i + (1-x_i)\log(1-\hat{x}_i)\right]$ | Per-pixel binary cross-entropy | Binary $\{0,1\}$ image data |
| $\Omega(z) = \sum_{j=1}^{d} \text{KL}(\rho \vert\vert \hat{\rho}_j)$ | Sparsity penalty (KL-divergence form) | Sparse autoencoders |
| $\mathcal{L}_{\text{total}} = \mathcal{L}(x, \hat{x}) + \lambda \Omega(z)$ | Regularized total loss | Sparse / contractive AEs |
