# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Encoder-Decoder Architecture: Detailed Operational Theory

### Operational Steps in a Seq2Seq Model

The complete operational flow consists of **three distinct phases** executed sequentially during inference and slightly modified during training:

#### Phase 1: Encoding Phase
The encoder RNN processes the input sequence one token at a time, updating its hidden state at each step. Mathematically, the encoder maintains a hidden state $\mathbf{h}_t^{enc}$ that is a function of the previous hidden state and the current input:

**Step 1.1:** Initialize the encoder hidden state: $\mathbf{h}_0^{enc} = \mathbf{0}$ (zero vector) or a learned initial state.

**Step 1.2:** For each time step $t = 1, 2, \ldots, T_x$, compute:

$$\mathbf{h}_t^{enc} = f_{enc}(\mathbf{W}_{xh} x_t + \mathbf{W}_{hh} \mathbf{h}_{t-1}^{enc} + \mathbf{b}_h)$$

**Step 1.3:** After processing the final input token $x_{T_x}$, form the context vector:

$$\mathbf{C} = \mathbf{h}_{T_x}^{enc}$$

The context vector $\mathbf{C}$ is the **only information** the decoder receives about the input. This bottleneck is both the strength (compressed representation) and weakness (information loss for long sequences) of the basic Seq2Seq model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Information Bottleneck Problem:** The fixed-size context vector $\mathbf{C}$ is the most significant limitation of the basic Seq2Seq model. For long input sequences, important information from early tokens is often "forgotten" by the encoder. This motivated the development of the **Attention Mechanism** (Bahdanau et al., 2014) and later **Transformers** (Vaswani et al., 2017), which allow the decoder to access ALL encoder hidden states, not just the final one.

#### Phase 2: Decoding Phase (Inference Mode)

**Step 2.1:** Initialize the decoder hidden state with the context vector: $\mathbf{h}_0^{dec} = \mathbf{C}$.

**Step 2.2:** Feed a special **start-of-sequence token** $\langle \text{SOS} \rangle$ as the first input $y_0$ to the decoder.

**Step 2.3:** For each decoding time step $t = 1, 2, \ldots$:

$$\mathbf{h}_t^{dec} = f_{dec}(\mathbf{W}_{yh} y_{t-1} + \mathbf{W}_{hh}^{dec} \mathbf{h}_{t-1}^{dec} + \mathbf{W}_{Ch} \mathbf{C} + \mathbf{b}_d)$$

$$\hat{y}_t = \text{softmax}(\mathbf{W}_{o} \mathbf{h}_t^{dec} + \mathbf{b}_o)$$

**Step 2.4:** Select the next token: $y_t = \arg\max(\hat{y}_t)$ (greedy) or use beam search.

**Step 2.5:** Stop when an **end-of-sequence token** $\langle \text{EOS} \rangle$ is generated.

#### Phase 3: Training Phase (Teacher Forcing)

During training, instead of feeding the decoder's own prediction $\hat{y}_{t-1}$ back as input, we feed the **ground-truth token** $y_{t-1}^{gt}$. This is called **Teacher Forcing** and drastically accelerates convergence by providing correct context at each step.

The training objective is the **negative log-likelihood** of the target sequence:

$$\mathcal{L}(\theta) = -\sum_{t=1}^{T_y} \log p(y_t^{gt} \mid y_1^{gt}, y_2^{gt}, \ldots, y_{t-1}^{gt}, \mathbf{C})$$

This loss is minimized via standard gradient descent (or Adam, RMSprop), and the gradients are computed using **BPTT** through the decoder's recurrent connections.

### Variant: Bidirectional Encoder

To capture context from BOTH directions of the input, the encoder can be **bidirectional**:

$$\overrightarrow{\mathbf{h}}_t^{enc} = f_{enc}^{\rightarrow}(\overrightarrow{\mathbf{h}}_{t-1}^{enc}, x_t)$$

$$\overleftarrow{\mathbf{h}}_t^{enc} = f_{enc}^{\leftarrow}(\overleftarrow{\mathbf{h}}_{t+1}^{enc}, x_t)$$

$$\mathbf{h}_t^{enc} = [\overrightarrow{\mathbf{h}}_t^{enc}; \overleftarrow{\mathbf{h}}_t^{enc}]$$

The context vector is then typically formed by concatenating the final forward state and the final backward state: $\mathbf{C} = [\overrightarrow{\mathbf{h}}_{T_x}^{enc}; \overleftarrow{\mathbf{h}}_1^{enc}]$.

---

## 2.2 BPTT: Detailed Operational Theory

### Unrolling the RNN

Consider a simple vanilla RNN with recurrence:

$$\mathbf{h}_t = \tanh(\mathbf{W}_{xh} x_t + \mathbf{W}_{hh} \mathbf{h}_{t-1} + \mathbf{b}_h)$$

$$\hat{y}_t = \text{softmax}(\mathbf{W}_{hy} \mathbf{h}_t + \mathbf{b}_y)$$

When we "unroll" this RNN over $T$ time steps, we create a deep feedforward network with $T$ layers where **all layers share the same weight matrices** $\mathbf{W}_{xh}, \mathbf{W}_{hh}, \mathbf{W}_{hy}$. Each "layer" corresponds to one time step.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Through Time"?** The name "Backpropagation Through Time" comes from the fact that the unrolled network's depth corresponds to the temporal dimension. We are applying standard backpropagation, but "through" the time dimension of the sequence. The chain rule must be applied across all $T$ time steps.

### The Core Gradient Computation

The total loss over the sequence is the sum of per-step losses:

$$L = \sum_{t=1}^{T} L_t(\hat{y}_t, y_t^{gt})$$

To update $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$, we need $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hh}}$. Using the chain rule, this gradient must account for the fact that $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$ affects $\mathbf{h}_t$ directly AND all subsequent hidden states $\mathbf{h}_{t+1}, \mathbf{h}_{t+2}, \ldots, \mathbf{h}_T$ through the recurrence.

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hh}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hh}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{k=1}^{t} \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_t} \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_k} \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_k}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hh}}$$

The crucial term is the "Jacobian across time":

$$\frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_k} = \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_i}{\partial \mathbf{h}_{i-1}} = \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} \mathbf{W}_{hh}^{\top} \text{diag}(1 - \tanh^2(\mathbf{W}_{hh} \mathbf{h}_{i-1} + \mathbf{b}_h))$$

This is a **product of $T$ Jacobian matrices**, and the behavior of this product is the source of the vanishing/exploding gradient problem.

### Vanishing vs Exploding Gradient

Let $\lambda_1 \geq \lambda_2 \geq \ldots \geq \lambda_n$ be the eigenvalues of $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$. For a tanh network:

$$\left\| \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_k} \right\| \approx \left(\lambda_{\max} \cdot \gamma \right)^{t-k}$$

where $\gamma \leq 1$ bounds the derivative of $\tanh$.

- **Vanishing:** If $\lambda_{\max} \cdot \gamma < 1$, then the gradient decays **exponentially** as $t - k$ grows. For long sequences, gradients from distant time steps vanish.
- **Exploding:** If $\lambda_{\max} \cdot \gamma > 1$, the gradient grows **exponentially**, causing numerical overflow and unstable training.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Focus:** Students must understand WHY vanishing gradients occur. The key insight is that the gradient is a product of Jacobian matrices, and repeated multiplication by matrices with spectral radius less than 1 (for vanishing) or greater than 1 (for exploding) leads to exponential decay or growth.

### Solutions to Vanishing/Exploding Gradients

| Solution | Mechanism | Targets | Trade-off |
|----------|-----------|---------|-----------|
| **LSTM** | Gating mechanism with memory cell | Vanishing | More parameters, slower training |
| **GRU** | Simplified gating (reset, update gates) | Vanishing | Fewer parameters than LSTM |
| **Gradient Clipping** | Rescale gradient if $\|\mathbf{g}\| > \tau$ | Exploding | Simple, no architecture change |
| **Orthogonal Initialization** | Initialize $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$ as orthogonal matrix | Both | Helps but not a complete fix |
| **Layer Normalization** | Normalize activations per time step | Both | Used in modern RNNs |
| **Truncated BPTT** | Limit backpropagation horizon to $k$ | Both | Approximate gradient |
| **Skip Connections** | Add direct paths between distant time steps | Vanishing | Architectural change |

**Gradient Clipping Formula (norm-based):**

$$\mathbf{g} \leftarrow \begin{cases} \mathbf{g} & \text{if } \|\mathbf{g}\| \leq \tau \\ \frac{\tau \mathbf{g}}{\|\mathbf{g}\|} & \text{if } \|\mathbf{g}\| > \tau \end{cases}$$

This is the **Pascanu et al. (2013)** method, the standard approach cited in deep learning textbooks.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Encoder hidden state update | $\mathbf{h}_t^{enc} = f(\mathbf{W}_{xh} x_t + \mathbf{W}_{hh} \mathbf{h}_{t-1}^{enc} + \mathbf{b}_h)$ | $x_t \in \mathbb{R}^{d_x}$, $\mathbf{h}_t \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h}$ |
| 2 | Context Vector | $\mathbf{C} = \mathbf{h}_{T_x}^{enc}$ | $\mathbf{C} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h}$ |
| 3 | Decoder hidden state update | $\mathbf{h}_t^{dec} = f(\mathbf{W}_{yh} y_{t-1} + \mathbf{W}_{hh}^{dec} \mathbf{h}_{t-1}^{dec} + \mathbf{W}_{Ch} \mathbf{C} + \mathbf{b}_d)$ | Conditional on $\mathbf{C}$ |
| 4 | Output probability | $\hat{y}_t = \text{softmax}(\mathbf{W}_o \mathbf{h}_t^{dec} + \mathbf{b}_o)$ | $\hat{y}_t \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert V \vert}$ over vocab |
| 5 | Seq2Seq loss (NLL) | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{t=1}^{T_y} \log p(y_t \mid y_{<t}, \mathbf{C})$ | Cross-entropy loss |
| 6 | Vanilla RNN cell | $\mathbf{h}_t = \tanh(\mathbf{W}_{xh} x_t + \mathbf{W}_{hh} \mathbf{h}_{t-1} + \mathbf{b}_h)$ | Easiest to analyze |
| 7 | BPTT gradient on $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$ | $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hh}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{k \leq t} \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_t} \left( \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_i}{\partial \mathbf{h}_{i-1}} \right) \frac{\partial^+ \mathbf{h}_k}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hh}}$ | The "$\frac{\partial^+}{\partial \mathbf{W}}$" denotes the direct (immediate) partial |
| 8 | Norm of gradient through time | $\left\| \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_k} \right\| \leq \left( \lambda_{\max} \cdot \gamma \right)^{t-k}$ | $\gamma$ = bound on activation derivative |
| 9 | Vanishing condition | $\lambda_{\max} \cdot \gamma < 1$ | Gradient $\to 0$ exponentially |
| 10 | Exploding condition | $\lambda_{\max} \cdot \gamma > 1$ | Gradient $\to \infty$ exponentially |
| 11 | Gradient clipping | $\mathbf{g} \leftarrow \frac{\tau \mathbf{g}}{\|\mathbf{g}\|}$ if $\|\mathbf{g}\| > \tau$ | Pascanu et al. 2013 |
| 12 | Teacher Forcing input | $y_{t-1}^{gt}$ replaces $\hat{y}_{t-1}$ | Training only |
| 13 | Bidirectional hidden state | $\mathbf{h}_t = [\overrightarrow{\mathbf{h}}_t; \overleftarrow{\mathbf{h}}_t]$ | Concatenation |
| 14 | Truncated BPTT horizon | $k$ time steps | Typical $k = 35$ |
| 15 | Per-step loss | $L_t = -\log p(y_t \mid y_{<t}, \mathbf{h}_t)$ | Cross-entropy |
| 16 | Total sequence loss | $L = \sum_{t=1}^{T} L_t$ | Additive over time |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE FOR TABLES:** The absolute value of a vocabulary $\vert V \vert$ uses `\vert` instead of `|` to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax. This same convention applies to norm symbols, determinants, and absolute values throughout these notes.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Architecture | Why It Matters |
|------------|--------------|----------------|
| Google Translate (2016–2020) | Seq2Seq + Attention | First NMT to surpass phrase-based statistical MT |
| Medical Report Generation | Encoder-Decoder CNN-RNN | X-ray $\to$ diagnostic text |
| Speech Recognition | Encoder-Decoder with Attention | Audio waveform $\to$ transcribed text |
| Video Captioning | CNN Encoder + RNN Decoder | Video frames $\to$ natural language |
| Time Series Forecasting | Encoder-Decoder LSTM | Multi-step prediction, anomaly detection |
| Chatbots / Dialogue Systems | Seq2Seq with attention | Conversational AI, customer support |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: BPTT Gradient for a Vanilla RNN

### Setup
Consider a vanilla RNN processing a sequence of length $T$ with:
- Input $x_t \in \mathbb{R}^{d_x}$
- Hidden state $h_t \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h}$
- Output $\hat{y}_t \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert V \vert}$
- Loss $L_t$ at each time step (cross-entropy)

**Recurrence equations:**

$$h_t = \tanh(\mathbf{W}_{xh} x_t + \mathbf{W}_{hh} h_{t-1} + b_h) = \tanh(\mathbf{a}_t)$$

where $\mathbf{a}_t = \mathbf{W}_{xh} x_t + \mathbf{W}_{hh} h_{t-1} + b_h$ is the pre-activation.

$$\hat{y}_t = \text{softmax}(\mathbf{W}_{hy} h_t + b_y)$$

**Total loss:**

$$L = \sum_{t=1}^{T} L_t$$

### Derivation Step 1: Gradient of Loss w.r.t. Output

We start from the top of the network. The gradient of $L_t$ with respect to the pre-softmax activations $o_t = \mathbf{W}_{hy} h_t + b_y$ is well-known for cross-entropy + softmax:

$$\frac{\partial L_t}{\partial o_t} = \hat{y}_t - y_t^{gt}$$

This is the **standard result** for softmax + cross-entropy and is a key identity students must memorize for KTU exams.

### Derivation Step 2: Gradient of Loss w.r.t. Hidden State at Time $t$

The hidden state $h_t$ affects the loss $L_t$ directly (through the output) AND affects all future losses $L_{t+1}, L_{t+2}, \ldots, L_T$ through the recurrence. We must sum all paths:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial h_t} = \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial h_t} + \sum_{i=t+1}^{T} \frac{\partial L_i}{\partial h_t}$$

For the direct contribution at time $t$:

$$\frac{\partial L_t}{\partial h_t} = \mathbf{W}_{hy}^{\top} \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial o_t} = \mathbf{W}_{hy}^{\top} (\hat{y}_t - y_t^{gt})$$

For the contribution from future time steps, we need the recursive relationship:

$$\frac{\partial L_{t+1}}{\partial h_t} = \frac{\partial L_{t+1}}{\partial h_{t+1}} \cdot \frac{\partial h_{t+1}}{\partial h_t}$$

### Derivation Step 3: The Local Jacobian $\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_{t-1}}$

Using $h_t = \tanh(\mathbf{a}_t)$ where $\mathbf{a}_t = \mathbf{W}_{xh} x_t + \mathbf{W}_{hh} h_{t-1} + b_h$:

$$\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_{t-1}} = \text{diag}(1 - \tanh^2(\mathbf{a}_t)) \cdot \mathbf{W}_{hh}^{\top} = \text{diag}(1 - h_t^2) \cdot \mathbf{W}_{hh}^{\top}$$

This Jacobian is the source of the vanishing/exploding gradient. The factor $\text{diag}(1 - h_t^2)$ has entries in $(0, 1)$ since $\tanh$ is bounded, but $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$ can have arbitrary spectral radius.

### Derivation Step 4: The Recursive Gradient Formula (BPTT Master Equation)

Combining the above, the gradient of the total loss $L$ with respect to $h_t$ is computed **recursively backward in time**:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial h_t} = \mathbf{W}_{hy}^{\top} (\hat{y}_t - y_t^{gt}) + \mathbf{W}_{hh}^{\top} \text{diag}(1 - h_{t+1}^2) \frac{\partial L}{\partial h_{t+1}}$$

In compact notation, define $\delta_t = \frac{\partial L}{\partial h_t}$. Then:

$$\delta_t = \mathbf{W}_{hy}^{\top} (\hat{y}_t - y_t^{gt}) + \mathbf{W}_{hh}^{\top} \text{diag}(1 - h_{t+1}^2) \delta_{t+1}$$

**Boundary condition:** At the final time step $T$, there is no future contribution:

$$\delta_T = \mathbf{W}_{hy}^{\top} (\hat{y}_T - y_T^{gt})$$

This is the **BPTT recurrence** — note the structural similarity to the forward pass, except it runs BACKWARD in time and accumulates gradients.

### Derivation Step 5: Gradient w.r.t. Recurrent Weights $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$

The gradient of the loss with respect to $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$ accumulates contributions from EVERY time step $t$:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hh}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \text{diag}(1 - h_t^2) \cdot \delta_t \cdot h_{t-1}^{\top}$$

Wait — let us be more careful. The gradient is:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hh}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{\partial L}{\partial h_t} \cdot \frac{\partial h_t}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hh}}$$

where the "immediate" derivative is $\frac{\partial^+ h_t}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hh}} = \text{diag}(1 - h_t^2) \cdot h_{t-1}^{\top}$.

The full gradient, using the BPTT $\delta_t$ definition:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hh}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \delta_t \cdot \left[\text{diag}(1 - h_t^2)\right] \cdot h_{t-1}^{\top}$$

This is a sum of $T$ outer-product-like terms, each of dimension $d_h \times d_h$.

### Derivation Step 6: Showing Vanishing Gradient

Apply the chain rule over $k$ time steps from $h_t$ back to $h_{t-k}$:

$$\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_{t-k}} = \prod_{i=t-k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial h_i}{\partial h_{i-1}} = \prod_{i=t-k+1}^{t} \text{diag}(1 - h_i^2) \mathbf{W}_{hh}^{\top}$$

Taking the spectral norm of both sides:

$$\left\| \frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_{t-k}} \right\| \leq \prod_{i=t-k+1}^{t} \| \text{diag}(1 - h_i^2) \| \cdot \|\mathbf{W}_{hh}^{\top}\| \leq \left( \lambda_{\max} \cdot \gamma \right)^k$$

where $\gamma = \max_i \| \text{diag}(1 - h_i^2) \| < 1$ for tanh activations.

**Conclusion:** If $\lambda_{\max} \cdot \gamma < 1$, the gradient vanishes exponentially in $k$, making it impossible to learn long-range dependencies.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Seq2Seq Training Loss and Gradients

### Setup
For a Seq2Seq model with encoder and decoder LSTMs, the loss is:

$$\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \sum_{t=1}^{T_y^{(n)}} -\log p_\theta(y_t^{(n)} \mid y_{<t}^{(n)}, \mathbf{C}^{(n)})$$

where $n$ indexes training examples in the dataset, and $\theta$ includes all encoder and decoder parameters.

### Forward Pass Derivation

**Step 1: Encoder forward pass (vectorized over time).**

For each time step $t = 1, \ldots, T_x$:

$$i_t = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_{xi} x_t + \mathbf{W}_{hi} h_{t-1} + b_i)$$

$$f_t = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_{xf} x_t + \mathbf{W}_{hf} h_{t-1} + b_f)$$

$$o_t = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_{xo} x_t + \mathbf{W}_{ho} h_{t-1} + b_o)$$

$$\tilde{c}_t = \tanh(\mathbf{W}_{xc} x_t + \mathbf{W}_{hc} h_{t-1} + b_c)$$

$$c_t = f_t \odot c_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{c}_t$$

$$h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(c_t)$$

**Step 2: Context vector formation.** $\mathbf{C} = h_{T_x}$ and initial cell state $c_0^{dec} = c_{T_x}$ (typical design choice).

**Step 3: Decoder forward pass (using teacher forcing).**

For each $t = 1, \ldots, T_y$, feed the ground-truth token $y_{t-1}^{gt}$ and compute the same LSTM equations to get $h_t^{dec}$, then:

$$\hat{y}_t = \text{softmax}(\mathbf{W}_{o} h_t^{dec} + b_o)$$

**Step 4: Per-step loss.** $L_t = -\sum_{k} y_t^{gt}[k] \log \hat{y}_t[k]$, simplified (since $y_t^{gt}$ is one-hot) to:

$$L_t = -\log \hat{y}_t[y_t^{gt}]$$

### Backward Pass Derivation (via BPTT)

The gradient of the total loss w.r.t. the decoder output projection:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}_o} = \sum_{t=1}^{T_y} (\hat{y}_t - y_t^{gt}) (h_t^{dec})^{\top}$$

The gradient propagates backward through the decoder LSTM, and the gradient w.r.t. the **initial decoder hidden state** $h_0^{dec} = \mathbf{C}$ tells us how the context vector should change:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{C}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial h_0^{dec}} = \text{(computed via BPTT through the decoder)}$$

This gradient is then backpropagated through the **encoder** to update encoder weights. This is why **BPTT is essential** for training Seq2Seq models — gradients must flow through BOTH the decoder and the encoder.

### Analytical Matrix: Comparison of BPTT Across Architectures

| Property | Vanilla RNN | LSTM | GRU | Seq2Seq |
|----------|-------------|------|-----|---------|
| Hidden state dim | $d_h$ | $d_h$ (also $d_c$) | $d_h$ | Encoder $d_h^e$, Decoder $d_h^d$ |
| Gradient path length | $T$ | $T$ (with cell highway) | $T$ (with update gate) | $T_x + T_y$ |
| Vanishing gradient | Severe | Mitigated | Mitigated | Mitigated |
| Exploding gradient | Possible | Possible | Possible | Possible |
| BPTT time complexity | $O(T \cdot d_h^2)$ | $O(T \cdot 4 d_h^2)$ | $O(T \cdot 3 d_h^2)$ | $O((T_x + T_y) d^2)$ |
| BPTT memory complexity | $O(T)$ | $O(T)$ | $O(T)$ | $O(T_x + T_y)$ |
| Truncation typical $k$ | 35 | 100 | 100 | 50–200 |

### Worked Numerical Example: Vanishing Gradient

**Problem:** A vanilla RNN with $d_h = 2$ has $\mathbf{W}_{hh} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.5 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.5 \end{pmatrix}$ and $\tanh$ activation. Compute the spectral radius of the gradient propagation operator.

**Solution:**

Step 1: The Jacobian of the recurrence is $\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_{t-1}} = \text{diag}(1 - h_t^2) \mathbf{W}_{hh}^{\top}$.

Step 2: For $\tanh$, the maximum value of $(1 - h_t^2)$ is 1, achieved at $h_t = 0$. Thus $\gamma = 1$.

Step 3: The eigenvalues of $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$ are $\lambda_1 = \lambda_2 = 0.5$. The spectral radius is $\lambda_{\max} = 0.5$.

Step 4: The product over $k$ time steps has norm $\leq (0.5 \cdot 1)^k = 0.5^k$.

Step 5: For $k = 10$: gradient norm shrinks by factor $0.5^{10} = 9.77 \times 10^{-4}$. For $k = 50$: factor $0.5^{50} \approx 8.88 \times 10^{-16}$ — essentially zero (vanishing!).

**Conclusion:** Even with this modest weight matrix, gradients vanish rapidly over time. This is the fundamental problem BPTT must contend with.

```mermaid
        x2["X2 token"]
        x3["X3 token"]

        e1["h1 enc"]
        e2["h2 enc"]
        e3["h3 enc"]
        e4["hT_x enc"]
    end

        ctx["C fixed size latent thought vector d dimensions"]
    end

    subgraph DEC["DECODER RNN LSTM GRU"]
        d0["h0 dec receives C and SOS token"]
        d1["h1 dec"]
        d2["h2 dec"]
        d3["h3 dec"]

    end

        y1["Y1 prediction"]
        y2["Y2 prediction"]
        y3["Y3 prediction"]
        y4["EOS end of sequence"]
    end

    x1 --> e1
    x2 --> e2
    x3 --> e3
    x4 --> e4

    e1 --> e2
    e2 --> e3
    e3 --> e4

    e4 --> ctx

    ctx --> d0
    d0 --> d1
    d1 --> d2
    d2 --> d3
    d3 --> d4

    d1 --> y1
    d2 --> y2
    d3 --> y3
    d4 --> y4

    y1 -. autoregressive feedback .-> d2
    y2 -. autoregressive feedback .-> d3
    y3 -. autoregressive feedback .-> d4

```

## 4.2 BPTT Unrolled Computational Graph

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph T0["TIME STEP t = 0"]
        h0["h0 initial state"]
    end

    subgraph T1["TIME STEP t = 1"]
        x1_in["x1 input"]
        W_xh["W_xh W_hh shared weights"]
        h1["h1 = tanh W_xh x1 + W_hh h0"]
        y1_out["y1 = softmax W_hy h1"]
        L1["L1 cross entropy loss"]
    end

    subgraph T2["TIME STEP t = 2"]
        x2_in["x2 input"]
        W_xh2["W_xh W_hh shared weights"]
        h2["h2 = tanh W_xh x2 + W_hh h1"]
        y2_out["y2 = softmax W_hy h2"]
        L2["L2 cross entropy loss"]
    end

    subgraph T3["TIME STEP t = 3"]
        x3_in["x3 input"]
        W_xh3["W_xh W_hh shared weights"]
        h3["h3 = tanh W_xh x3 + W_hh h2"]
        y3_out["y3 = softmax W_hy h3"]
        L3["L3 cross entropy loss"]
    end

    subgraph TN["TIME STEP t = T"]
        hT["hT final state"]
        yT["yT = softmax W_hy hT"]
        LT["LT cross entropy loss"]
    end

    subgraph BP["BACKWARD PASS BPTT"]
        dL_dWhh["dL_dW_hh = SUM_t delta_t h_t-1 transposed"]
        vanish["Vanishing if spectral radius less than 1"]
        explode["Exploding if spectral radius greater than 1"]
        clip["Gradient clipping if norm g greater than tau"]
    end

    h0 --> h1
    h1 --> h2
    h2 --> h3
    h3 --> hT

    x1_in --> h1
    x2_in --> h2
    x3_in --> h3

    h1 --> y1_out --> L1
    h2 --> y2_out --> L2
    h3 --> y3_out --> L3
    hT --> yT --> LT

    L1 --> dL_dWhh
    L2 --> dL_dWhh
    L3 --> dL_dWhh
    LT --> dL_dWhh

    dL_dWhh --> vanish
    dL_dWhh --> explode
    dL_dWhh --> clip

```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Seq2Seq Training with BPTT

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A["DATA PREPARATION STAGE"]
        A1["Input sequence X x1 x2 ... xTx"]
        A2["Target sequence Y y1 y2 ... yTy"]
        A3["Vocabulary embedding layer"]
    end

    subgraph B["ENCODER FORWARD PASS"]
        B1["Initialize h0 enc = zeros"]
        B2["For t = 1 to Tx compute h_t enc"]
        B3["Form context vector C = h_Tx enc"]
        B4["Save all hidden states for attention if used"]
    end

    subgraph C["DECODER FORWARD PASS TEACHER FORCING"]
        C1["Initialize h0 dec with C"]
        C2["Feed SOS token as y0"]
        C3["For t = 1 to Ty compute h_t dec and y_hat_t"]
        C4["Use ground truth y_t_gt as next input"]
        C5["Compute softmax over vocabulary"]
    end

    subgraph D["LOSS COMPUTATION"]
        D1["Per step loss L_t = minus log p y_t_gt"]
        D2["Total loss L = sum over t of L_t"]
    end

    subgraph E["BPTT BACKWARD PASS"]
        E1["Compute delta_T at final time step"]
        E2["Recurse delta_t backward through time"]
        E3["Accumulate gradient dL_dW_hh over all t"]
        E4["Backprop through encoder via dL_dC"]
        E5["Check gradient norm g for explosion"]
    end

    subgraph F["PARAMETER UPDATE"]
        F1["Apply gradient clipping if norm g greater than tau"]
        F2["Update W_hh, W_xh, W_hy using Adam or SGD"]
        F3["Repeat for all mini batches and epochs"]
    end

    A1 --> A3
    A2 --> A3
    A3 --> B2

    B3 --> C1
    B2 --> B4

    D2 --> E1

    E2 --> E3
    E3 --> E4
    E3 --> E5
    E5 --> F1
    F1 --> F2
    F2 --> F3
    F3 -. next epoch .-> B1
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Seq2Seq Context Vector Bottleneck
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Expected Pattern)]**

**Q: Explain the role of the context vector $\mathbf{C}$ in an Encoder-Decoder Seq2Seq model. What is the "information bottleneck" problem and how does the attention mechanism address it?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **context vector** $\mathbf{C}$ is the final hidden state of the encoder RNN that serves as a compressed summary of the entire input sequence. It is the **only** information passed from the encoder to the decoder, and from it the decoder must generate the complete output sequence.

The **information bottleneck** problem arises because $\mathbf{C}$ is of fixed dimension $d$, regardless of the input sequence length $T_x$. For long input sequences (e.g., $T_x = 100$ words), the encoder must compress all that information into a single fixed-size vector, which causes:
1. **Loss of information** from early tokens (forgotten by the final state).
2. **Difficulty learning long-range dependencies**.

The **attention mechanism** (Bahdanau et al., 2014) addresses this by allowing the decoder to access **all** encoder hidden states $\{\mathbf{h}_1^{enc}, \mathbf{h}_2^{enc}, \ldots, \mathbf{h}_{T_x}^{enc}\}$ at every decoding step, not just the final one. The decoder computes a weighted sum of encoder states, with attention weights $\alpha_t$ dynamically determined by relevance. This eliminates the bottleneck and dramatically improves performance on long sequences.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Scheme:** 1 mark for context vector definition, 1 mark for bottleneck problem, 1 mark for attention solution.

---

### Question 2: BPTT Vanishing Gradient
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**Q: Why does the vanishing gradient problem occur in BPTT, and what architectural solution is most commonly used to address it in modern RNNs?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **vanishing gradient problem** in BPTT occurs because the gradient with respect to the recurrent weight $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$ involves a **product of $T$ Jacobian matrices** across time:

$$\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_{t-k}} = \prod_{i=t-k+1}^{t} \text{diag}(1 - h_i^2) \mathbf{W}_{hh}^{\top}$$

If the spectral radius of $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$ satisfies $\lambda_{\max} \cdot \gamma < 1$ (where $\gamma < 1$ for tanh activations), this product **decays exponentially** with the time gap $k$, causing gradients from distant time steps to vanish. This makes it impossible for the network to learn long-range dependencies.

The most common architectural solution is the **Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)** cell, which uses a **gating mechanism** (input gate, forget gate, output gate) and a **memory cell** $c_t$ with an **additive** update rule:

$$c_t = f_t \odot c_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{c}_t$$

The additive update (rather than multiplicative) creates a "gradient highway" that allows gradients to flow across many time steps without vanishing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Scheme:** 1 mark for explaining the product-of-Jacobians structure, 1 mark for the exponential decay condition, 1 mark for LSTM's gating/memory cell solution.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**Q: (a)** Describe the Encoder-Decoder Sequence-to-Sequence architecture in detail. Draw the block diagram and explain the role of the encoder, decoder, and context vector. Discuss the training procedure with teacher forcing. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the BPTT gradient update rule for the recurrent weight matrix $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$ of a vanilla RNN. Show clearly how the vanishing gradient problem arises and discuss TWO techniques to mitigate it. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Part A(a) — 7 Marks

**Architecture Overview (2 marks):**

The Seq2Seq architecture consists of two coupled RNNs:

1. **Encoder RNN**: Processes the input sequence $\mathbf{X} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_{T_x})$ one token at a time, maintaining a hidden state that captures the cumulative meaning. The encoder is typically an LSTM or GRU.

2. **Context Vector $\mathbf{C}$**: The final hidden state of the encoder, $\mathbf{C} = h_{T_x}^{enc}$, which acts as a fixed-size summary of the input.

3. **Decoder RNN**: Initialized with $\mathbf{C}$ as its initial hidden state, the decoder generates the output sequence $\mathbf{Y} = (y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_{T_y})$ one token at a time in an **autoregressive** manner.

**Mathematical Operations (2 marks):**

**Encoder forward pass:**
$$h_t^{enc} = \tanh(\mathbf{W}_{xh}^e x_t + \mathbf{W}_{hh}^e h_{t-1}^{enc} + b_h^e), \quad t = 1, \ldots, T_x$$

**Context formation:** $\mathbf{C} = h_{T_x}^{enc}$.

**Decoder forward pass (with teacher forcing):**
$$h_t^{dec} = \tanh(\mathbf{W}_{yh}^d y_{t-1}^{gt} + \mathbf{W}_{hh}^d h_{t-1}^{dec} + \mathbf{W}_{Ch}^d \mathbf{C} + b_h^d)$$

$$\hat{y}_t = \text{softmax}(\mathbf{W}_o h_t^{dec} + b_o)$$

**Training with Teacher Forcing (2 marks):**

During training, the decoder's input at time $t$ is the **ground-truth token** $y_{t-1}^{gt}$ from the training set, NOT the decoder's own previous prediction $\hat{y}_{t-1}$. This is called **teacher forcing** and is essential for stable, fast convergence.

The loss is the **negative log-likelihood** of the target sequence:

$$\mathcal{L}(\theta) = -\sum_{t=1}^{T_y} \log p_\theta(y_t^{gt} \mid y_{<t}^{gt}, \mathbf{C})$$

This is minimized by **stochastic gradient descent** (or Adam/RMSprop) using gradients computed via BPTT.

**Block Diagram (1 mark):** See SECTION 4.1 for the diagram, which must be drawn in the answer script.

---

#### Model Solution to Part A(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Setup of the RNN (1 mark):**

For a vanilla RNN with hidden state $h_t \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h}$:

$$h_t = \tanh(\mathbf{a}_t), \quad \mathbf{a}_t = \mathbf{W}_{xh} x_t + \mathbf{W}_{hh} h_{t-1} + b_h$$

$$\hat{y}_t = \text{softmax}(\mathbf{W}_{hy} h_t + b_y)$$

Total loss: $L = \sum_{t=1}^{T} L_t$ where $L_t$ is the cross-entropy at time $t$.

**Step 2: Compute the Local Jacobian (1 mark):**

$$\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_{t-1}} = \text{diag}(1 - \tanh^2(\mathbf{a}_t)) \mathbf{W}_{hh}^{\top} = \text{diag}(1 - h_t^2) \mathbf{W}_{hh}^{\top}$$

**Step 3: Apply Chain Rule over Time (2 marks):**

To compute $\frac{\partial L_t}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hh}}$, we sum contributions from all paths through which $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$ at time $k$ affects the loss at time $t$ (for $k \leq t$):

$$\frac{\partial L_t}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hh}} = \sum_{k=1}^{t} \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial h_t} \cdot \frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_k} \cdot \frac{\partial^+ h_k}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hh}}$$

The product of Jacobians across $k$ time steps:

$$\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_k} = \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial h_i}{\partial h_{i-1}} = \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} \text{diag}(1 - h_i^2) \mathbf{W}_{hh}^{\top}$$

**Step 4: Total Gradient (1 mark):**

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hh}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{k=1}^{t} \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial h_t} \left[\prod_{i=k+1}^{t} \text{diag}(1 - h_i^2) \mathbf{W}_{hh}^{\top}\right] \frac{\partial^+ h_k}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hh}}$$

**Step 5: Show Vanishing Gradient (1 mark):**

Taking the spectral norm of the product:

$$\left\| \frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_k} \right\| \leq \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} \|\text{diag}(1 - h_i^2)\| \cdot \|\mathbf{W}_{hh}^{\top}\| \leq (\lambda_{\max} \cdot \gamma)^{t-k}$$

where $\lambda_{\max}$ is the largest singular value of $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$ and $\gamma = \max_i \|\text{diag}(1 - h_i^2)\| < 1$. For long-range dependencies ($t - k$ large) and $\lambda_{\max} \cdot \gamma < 1$, this product vanishes exponentially.

**Step 6: Two Mitigation Techniques (1 mark):**

1. **LSTM with Gating**: The cell state $c_t$ uses an additive update $c_t = f_t \odot c_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{c}_t$, which provides a near-linear gradient path through time. The forget gate $f_t \approx 1$ allows gradients to flow unimpeded.

2. **Gradient Clipping (Pascanu et al., 2013)**: Rescale gradients when $\|\mathbf{g}\| > \tau$ via $\mathbf{g} \leftarrow \tau \mathbf{g} / \|\mathbf{g}\|$. This addresses the EXPLODING gradient, while LSTM/GRU address the VANISHING gradient.

(Other acceptable answers: GRU, Orthogonal initialization, Truncated BPTT, Layer Normalization.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper 2024 Scheme, Alternative]**

**Q: (a)** With neat diagrams, explain the Encoder-Decoder Seq2Seq architecture. Discuss the limitations of the basic model and explain how the **Attention Mechanism** overcomes them. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the BPTT algorithm with the help of an unrolled computational graph. Derive the recursive gradient formula for $\delta_t = \frac{\partial L}{\partial h_t}$ and show how it leads to vanishing/exploding gradients. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Part B(a) — 7 Marks

**Architecture Explanation (3 marks):**

The Seq2Seq model has two RNN components: an **Encoder** that maps a variable-length input sequence $(x_1, \ldots, x_{T_x})$ to a fixed-dimensional context vector $\mathbf{C}$, and a **Decoder** that maps $\mathbf{C}$ to a variable-length output sequence $(y_1, \ldots, y_{T_y})$.

**Encoder equations:**

$$h_t^{enc} = \text{LSTM}(x_t, h_{t-1}^{enc}), \quad t = 1, \ldots, T_x$$
$$\mathbf{C} = h_{T_x}^{enc}$$

**Decoder equations (inference):**

$$h_t^{dec} = \text{LSTM}(y_{t-1}, h_{t-1}^{dec}, \mathbf{C})$$
$$\hat{y}_t = \text{softmax}(W_o h_t^{dec})$$

**Limitations of Basic Seq2Seq (2 marks):**

1. **Information Bottleneck:** $\mathbf{C}$ is fixed-size, so long inputs lose information.
2. **Equal Treatment of All Input Tokens:** The decoder cannot "focus" on the most relevant parts of the input for each output token.
3. **Long-Range Dependencies:** Difficult to learn for sequences longer than ~20 tokens.

**Attention Mechanism Solution (2 marks):**

Instead of a single context vector, attention computes a **dynamic context** $c_t^{dec}$ for each decoding time step $t$:

$$c_t^{dec} = \sum_{i=1}^{T_x} \alpha_{t,i} \cdot h_i^{enc}$$

where the attention weights are computed via a softmax over a compatibility score $e_{t,i}$:

$$e_{t,i} = \text{score}(h_{t-1}^{dec}, h_i^{enc}), \quad \alpha_{t,i} = \frac{\exp(e_{t,i})}{\sum_j \exp(e_{t,j})}$$

This allows the decoder to "attend" to different parts of the input at each output step, eliminating the bottleneck.

---

#### Model Solution to Part B(b) — 7 Marks

**Unrolled Graph Explanation (2 marks):**

The RNN is unrolled across $T$ time steps, creating a deep feedforward-like graph. The same weight matrices $\mathbf{W}_{xh}, \mathbf{W}_{hh}, \mathbf{W}_{hy}$ are SHARED across all $T$ "layers" (time steps). The unrolled graph is necessary because standard backpropagation requires a feedforward (DAG) structure.

**Recursive Gradient Derivation (3 marks):**

The total loss is $L = \sum_{t=1}^{T} L_t$. The hidden state $h_t$ affects the loss directly at time $t$ AND indirectly through all future times $t+1, \ldots, T$. Define:

$$\delta_t \equiv \frac{\partial L}{\partial h_t}$$

The recursion: $\delta_t$ has two contributions — the direct loss at time $t$ AND the future loss propagated backward through $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$:

$$\delta_t = \underbrace{\mathbf{W}_{hy}^{\top} (\hat{y}_t - y_t^{gt})}_{\text{direct contribution at time } t} + \underbrace{\mathbf{W}_{hh}^{\top} \text{diag}(1 - h_{t+1}^2) \delta_{t+1}}_{\text{future contribution propagated backward}}$$

**Boundary condition:** At the final step, $\delta_T = \mathbf{W}_{hy}^{\top} (\hat{y}_T - y_T^{gt})$.

**Vanishing/Exploding (2 marks):**

Unrolling the recursion from $\delta_t$ back to $\delta_k$ for $k < t$:

$$\delta_t \approx \left[\prod_{i=k+1}^{t} \text{diag}(1 - h_i^2) \mathbf{W}_{hh}^{\top}\right] \delta_k^{\text{local}}$$

The norm grows or decays exponentially:

$$\|\delta_t\| \approx (\lambda_{\max} \cdot \gamma)^{t-k} \|\delta_k^{\text{local}}\|$$

- If $\lambda_{\max} \cdot \gamma < 1$: **vanishing** — distant past has no influence on current updates.
- If $\lambda_{\max} \cdot \gamma > 1$: **exploding** — numerical instability.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Vanishing Gradient" with "Underfitting":** Vanishing gradient is a TRAINING problem, not a model capacity problem. The model may have enough capacity, but BPTT cannot deliver the gradient signal to early time steps.
>
> 2. **Forgetting Teacher Forcing in Training:** Students often describe the decoder as using its own prediction as input. In TRAINING, we use ground-truth tokens (teacher forcing). In INFERENCE, we use the model's own predictions. This distinction is worth 1–2 marks in KTU exams.
>
> 3. **Mixing Up Forward and Backward Passes:** The forward pass goes $\mathbf{X} \to h_1 \to h_2 \to \ldots \to h_T \to \hat{y}$, while the backward pass (BPTT) computes $\delta_T \to \delta_{T-1} \to \ldots \to \delta_1$. Students sometimes draw them in the same direction — this is a common error.
>
> 4. **Omitting the Spectral Radius Argument:** For full marks on vanishing gradient derivations, you MUST mention the spectral radius of $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$ and its role in exponential decay/growth.
>
> 5. **Confusing "Truncated BPTT" with "Mini-batch":** Truncated BPTT limits the BACKWARD PASS horizon to $k$ steps; the forward pass can still run longer. This is distinct from mini-batch SGD.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Quick-Reference Summary Checklist

- [x] **Seq2Seq = Encoder + Context Vector + Decoder**
- [x] **Encoder** reads input, produces context vector $\mathbf{C}$ = final hidden state
- [x] **Decoder** initialized with $\mathbf{C}$, generates output autoregressively
- [x] **Teacher Forcing** uses ground-truth tokens in training, NOT predictions
- [x] **Basic Seq2Seq** has an information bottleneck (fixed-size $\mathbf{C}$)
- [x] **Attention** solves the bottleneck by attending to all encoder states
- [x] **BPTT** is backprop on the unrolled RNN graph across $T$ time steps
- [x] **Shared weights** $\mathbf{W}_{xh}, \mathbf{W}_{hh}, \mathbf{W}_{hy}$ across all time steps
- [x] **Vanishing gradient** = spectral radius of $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$ < 1
- [x] **Exploding gradient** = spectral radius of $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$ > 1
- [x] **LSTM/GRU** solve vanishing via gating + additive cell updates
- [x] **Gradient clipping** solves exploding via norm-based rescaling
- [x] **Truncated BPTT** limits backward horizon to $k$ steps for efficiency
- [x] **Bidirectional encoder** reads input in both directions for richer context
- [x] **Beam search** is an inference heuristic alternative to greedy decoding

### Key Equations to Memorize

$$\boxed{h_t^{enc} = f(\mathbf{W}_{xh} x_t + \mathbf{W}_{hh} h_{t-1}^{enc} + b_h)}$$

$$\boxed{\mathbf{C} = h_{T_x}^{enc}}$$

$$\boxed{\hat{y}_t = \text{softmax}(\mathbf{W}_o h_t^{dec} + b_o)}$$

$$\boxed{\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{t=1}^{T_y} \log p(y_t \mid y_{<t}, \mathbf{C})}$$

$$\boxed{\delta_t = \mathbf{W}_{hy}^{\top} (\hat{y}_t - y_t^{gt}) + \mathbf{W}_{hh}^{\top} \text{diag}(1 - h_{t+1}^2) \delta_{t+1}}$$

$$\boxed{\left\| \frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_k} \right\| \leq (\lambda_{\max} \cdot \gamma)^{t-k}}$$

$$\boxed{\mathbf{g} \leftarrow \frac{\tau \mathbf{g}}{\|\mathbf{g}\|} \text{ if } \|\mathbf{g}\| > \tau}$$

### Historical Context (for KTU exam references)

- **2014 — Sutskever et al. & Cho et al.**: Original Seq2Seq model for machine translation
- **2014 — Bahdanau et al.**: Additive attention mechanism, solving the bottleneck
- **2013 — Pascanu et al.**: Formal analysis of vanishing/exploding gradients in RNNs
- **2010 — Mikolov et al.**: Truncated BPTT algorithm with $\tau_1, \tau_2$ parameters
- **2014 — Chung et al.**: Empirical comparison of LSTM vs GRU gating
- **2017 — Vaswani et al.**: "Attention is All You Need" — Transformers retire RNNs for most sequence tasks

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FINAL EXAM TIP:** For KTU 14-mark questions, ALWAYS include: (1) a clear diagram, (2) the relevant equations, (3) a worked derivation showing the chain rule, (4) a discussion of vanishing/exploding gradient, and (5) a real-world application. These five elements consistently score full marks.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

**Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)** is a specialized variant of Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) architecture, introduced by *Sepp Hochreiter & Jürgen Schmidhuber (1997)*, engineered specifically to overcome the **vanishing and exploding gradient problem** inherent in standard RNNs during Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT). 

LSTM introduces a **memory cell** regulated by three multiplicative **gating mechanisms** (forget, input, and output gates) that explicitly control the flow, retention, and erasure of information across long temporal sequences.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The core architectural unit of LSTM is the **memory cell state** $C_t$, which acts as a high-speed information conveyor belt traversing the entire sequence with only minor linear interactions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **smart whiteboard in a conference room** that has three assistants:

1. **The Forget Gate Assistant (Mr. Cleaner)** — Decides *what old notes to erase* from the whiteboard before writing new ones. If a piece of information is no longer useful, he wipes it out. 
2. **The Input Gate Assistant (Mr. Writer)** — Decides *what new information* to write on the whiteboard and exactly where to place it. He also decides *how strongly* to write it.
3. **The Output Gate Assistant (Mr. Speaker)** — Decides *what to read aloud* from the whiteboard to the audience next. He filters the stored notes and picks only the relevant ones to share.

The **whiteboard itself** is the **cell state** $C_t$ — it carries memory across time. The three assistants work in parallel using **sigmoid $\sigma$ (decision maker: 0 or 1)** and **tanh (value shaper: $-1$ to $+1$)** functions. This elegant division of labor allows LSTM to remember information for thousands of time-steps, which a vanilla RNN cannot do.

## 1.3 Core Components at a Glance

| Component | Symbol | Activation | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Forget Gate | $f_t$ | $\sigma$ | Decides what to discard from cell state |
| Input Gate | $i_t$ | $\sigma$ | Decides which values to update |
| Candidate Values | $\tilde{C}_t$ | $\tanh$ | Creates new candidate information |
| Cell State | $C_t$ | — | Long-term memory conveyor |
| Output Gate | $o_t$ | $\sigma$ | Decides what to output from cell state |
| Hidden State | $h_t$ | $\tanh$ | Short-term / working memory output |

> [!NOTE]
> The **sigmoid** function $\sigma$ outputs values in $[0, 1]$ — interpreted as *"how much"* to let through. The **tanh** function outputs in $[-1, 1]$ — used to *regulate* the actual values flowing through the network.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of LSTM — Structured Logic Flow

The LSTM unit processes an input vector $x_t$ at time-step $t$ and the previous hidden state $h_{t-1}$ to produce the current hidden state $h_t$ and updated cell state $C_t$. The operations occur in the following rigorous sequence:

### Step 1 — **Forget Gate Activation**
- **Why?** To determine how much of the *past cell state* should be retained. The sigmoid squashes the linear combination of previous hidden state and current input into $[0,1]$.
- **How?** A value close to $0$ means *"forget everything"*, while $1$ means *"keep everything"*.

$$f_t = \sigma(W_f \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f)$$

### Step 2 — **Input Gate Activation & Candidate Generation**
- **Why?** To identify *new information* worth storing. Two parallel computations happen here:
  - The **input gate** $i_t$ decides *which values to update*.
  - The **candidate vector** $\tilde{C}_t$ creates *new candidate values* to potentially add to the state.
- **How?** $i_t$ acts as a filter (via sigmoid), while $\tilde{C}_t$ shapes the candidate values (via tanh).

$$i_t = \sigma(W_i \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i)$$

$$\tilde{C}_t = \tanh(W_C \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_C)$$

### Step 3 — **Cell State Update (The Core Memory Operation)**
- **Why?** This is the heart of LSTM — it actually updates the long-term memory by combining:
  - The *old state*, filtered by the forget gate (selective erasure).
  - The *new candidates*, filtered by the input gate (selective addition).
- **How?** Element-wise multiplication $\odot$ ensures per-dimension gating; this linear update pathway is precisely what prevents gradient vanishing.

$$C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$$

### Step 4 — **Output Gate Activation**
- **Why?** To decide what part of the (newly updated) cell state will be emitted as the output / next hidden state.
- **How?** The output gate filters via sigmoid; then tanh compresses the cell state to $[-1, 1]$.

$$o_t = \sigma(W_o \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o)$$

$$h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$$

## 2.2 The "Why" — Solving the Vanishing Gradient Problem

In vanilla RNNs, the gradient of the loss with respect to hidden states involves repeated multiplication by the recurrent weight matrix $W$. For long sequences, this matrix power $\to 0$ (vanishing) or $\to \infty$ (exploding).

LSTM's cell state update uses **addition** ($+$) rather than **multiplication** between successive states:

$$C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$$

This additive structure allows the gradient to flow backward through time with much greater stability, since the partial derivative $\partial C_t / \partial C_{t-1}$ reduces to $f_t$ (a bounded vector), avoiding exponential decay.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Use Case |
|---|---|
| **Natural Language Processing (NLP)** | Machine translation, sentiment analysis, text generation |
| **Speech Recognition** | Acoustic modeling in ASR systems (Google Voice, Siri) |
| **Time-Series Forecasting** | Stock price prediction, weather modeling, energy load |
| **Bioinformatics** | Protein sequence prediction, DNA motif discovery |
| **Anomaly Detection** | Industrial IoT sensor streams, ECG/EEG signal analysis |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notation Convention:** All gates use the concatenated vector $[h_{t-1}, x_t]$ as input. The weight matrices $W_f, W_i, W_C, W_o$ have shape $(n, m+n)$ where $n$ = hidden size, $m$ = input size.

| # | Equation | Description | Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_t = \sigma(W_f [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f)$ | Forget Gate | $f_t \in [0,1]^n$ |
| 2 | $i_t = \sigma(W_i [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i)$ | Input Gate | $i_t \in [0,1]^n$ |
| 3 | $\tilde{C}_t = \tanh(W_C [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_C)$ | Candidate Cell | $\tilde{C}_t \in [-1,1]^n$ |
| 4 | $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ | Cell State Update | $C_t \in \mathbb{R}^n$ |
| 5 | $o_t = \sigma(W_o [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o)$ | Output Gate | $o_t \in [0,1]^n$ |
| 6 | $h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$ | Hidden State Output | $h_t \in [-1,1]^n$ |
| 7 | $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | Sigmoid Activation | $\sigma \in (0,1)$ |
| 8 | $\tanh(z) = \frac{e^{z} - e^{-z}}{e^{z} + e^{-z}}$ | Tanh Activation | $\tanh \in (-1,1)$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Bias Initialisation Tip (Examiners Love This):** Forget gate biases $b_f$ are often initialised to **+1** (or a small positive value like 0.01) to encourage the network to *retain* information initially — without this, training loss tends to diverge.

## 2.5 Variants of LSTM (Frequently Asked in KTU)

- **Peephole LSTM**: Gates also receive input from the cell state $C_{t-1}$.
- **Coupled Forget-Input Gate**: Forget and input gates are coupled ($i_t = 1 - f_t$).
- **Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU)**: Simplified version combining forget + input into a single "update gate" and merging cell state with hidden state.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Forward Pass — Full Exhaustive Derivation

Given input $x_t \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$, previous hidden state $h_{t-1} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$, and previous cell state $C_{t-1} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$:

**Step (a): Concatenation Operation**

We form the joint input vector by stacking $h_{t-1}$ and $x_t$ vertically:

$$z_t = \begin{bmatrix} h_{t-1} \\ x_t \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{(n+m)}$$

**Step (b): Linear Projection for Each Gate**

Each gate has its own learnable weight matrix $W \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times (n+m)}$ and bias $b \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$. The pre-activation for each gate is:

$$\text{net}_f = W_f z_t + b_f, \quad \text{net}_i = W_i z_t + b_i$$

$$\text{net}_C = W_C z_t + b_C, \quad \text{net}_o = W_o z_t + b_o$$

**Step (c): Apply Non-Linearities**

$$f_t = \sigma(\text{net}_f), \quad i_t = \sigma(\text{net}_i)$$

$$\tilde{C}_t = \tanh(\text{net}_C), \quad o_t = \sigma(\text{net}_o)$$

**Step (d): Cell State Update**

$$C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$$

*(Here, $\odot$ is the Hadamard / element-wise product.)*

**Step (e): Hidden State Output**

$$h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$$

**Step (f): Output Prediction (for classification / regression tasks)**

$$\hat{y}_t = \text{softmax}(W_y h_t + b_y) \quad \text{or} \quad \hat{y}_t = W_y h_t + b_y$$

## 3.2 Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) — Gradient Derivations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> LSTM training uses **BPTT**. The total loss over a sequence of length $T$ is the sum of per-step losses: $L = \sum_{t=1}^{T} L_t(\hat{y}_t, y_t)$. We compute $\partial L / \partial W$ by chaining through all time steps.

### 3.2.1 Gradient of Loss w.r.t. Hidden State

The hidden state at time $t$ affects:
- The loss $L_t$ directly.
- All future cell states $C_{t+1}, C_{t+2}, \dots, C_T$ (via the gate computations).

Let $\delta h_t = \partial L / \partial h_t$ (the gradient flowing into $h_t$ from both the immediate loss and future states).

### 3.2.2 Gradient of Loss w.r.t. Cell State

The cell state $C_t$ is influenced by:
- Future cell states $C_{t+1}$ (via the forget gate multiplication).
- Future hidden states $h_t$ (via $\tanh(C_t)$ inside the output gate).

$$\delta C_t = \frac{\partial L}{\partial C_t} = \delta h_t \odot o_t \odot (1 - \tanh^2(C_t)) + \delta C_{t+1} \odot f_{t+1}$$

**Logic:** The first term comes from $\partial h_t / \partial C_t = o_t \odot (1 - \tanh^2(C_t))$ using the derivative of $\tanh$. The second term propagates from the future cell state via the forget gate multiplication $C_{t+1} = f_{t+1} \odot C_t + (\dots)$.

### 3.2.3 Gradient of Loss w.r.t. Output Gate

The output gate $o_t$ affects only $h_t$ (since $h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$):

$$\delta o_t = \frac{\partial L}{\partial o_t} = \delta h_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$$

Now, to update $W_o$, we multiply by the derivative of the sigmoid:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_o} = \delta o_t \odot o_t \odot (1 - o_t) \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t]^T$$

### 3.2.4 Gradient of Loss w.r.t. Cell State Pre-Activation (via $\tilde{C}_t$)

$$\delta \tilde{C}_t = \delta C_t \odot i_t$$

Applying tanh derivative:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_C} = \delta \tilde{C}_t \odot (1 - \tilde{C}_t^2) \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t]^T$$

### 3.2.5 Gradient of Loss w.r.t. Input Gate

The input gate $i_t$ affects only the cell state $C_t$ via the $\odot \tilde{C}_t$ term:

$$\delta i_t = \delta C_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$$

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_i} = \delta i_t \odot i_t \odot (1 - i_t) \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t]^T$$

### 3.2.6 Gradient of Loss w.r.t. Forget Gate

The forget gate $f_t$ affects $C_t$ via $f_t \odot C_{t-1}$:

$$\delta f_t = \delta C_t \odot C_{t-1}$$

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_f} = \delta f_t \odot f_t \odot (1 - f_t) \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t]^T$$

### 3.2.7 Gradient of Loss w.r.t. Previous Hidden State (for BPTT roll-back)

The hidden state $h_{t-1}$ is the input to *all four* gates at time $t$. Therefore:

$$\delta h_{t-1} = W_f^T \delta f_{\text{raw}} + W_i^T \delta i_{\text{raw}} + W_C^T \delta \tilde{C}_{\text{raw}} + W_o^T \delta o_{\text{raw}}$$

where $\delta_{\text{raw}}$ denotes pre-activation gradients (before applying the activation derivative).

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** Suppose $h_{t-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ -0.2 \end{bmatrix}$, $x_t = \begin{bmatrix} 1.0 \\ 0.3 \end{bmatrix}$, $C_{t-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.8 \\ -0.4 \end{bmatrix}$, and all weights/biases are identity / zero.

**Step 1: Concatenate** $z_t = [0.5, -0.2, 1.0, 0.3]^T$

**Step 2: Gates** (with $W=I$, $b=0$, so $f_t = i_t = o_t = \sigma(z_t)$ and $\tilde{C}_t = \tanh(z_t)$)

$$\sigma(0.5) \approx 0.622, \quad \sigma(-0.2) \approx 0.450, \quad \sigma(1.0) \approx 0.731, \quad \sigma(0.3) \approx 0.574$$

$$f_t \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.622 \\ 0.450 \end{bmatrix}, \quad i_t \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.731 \\ 0.574 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\tanh(0.5) \approx 0.462, \quad \tanh(-0.2) \approx -0.197, \quad \tanh(1.0) \approx 0.762, \quad \tanh(0.3) \approx 0.291$$

$$\tilde{C}_t \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.462 \\ -0.197 \end{bmatrix}, \quad o_t \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.731 \\ 0.574 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Cell State Update**

$$C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$$

For dimension 1: $C_t^{(1)} = 0.622 \times 0.8 + 0.731 \times 0.462 = 0.4976 + 0.3377 = 0.8353$

For dimension 2: $C_t^{(2)} = 0.450 \times (-0.4) + 0.574 \times (-0.197) = -0.180 + (-0.113) = -0.293$

$$C_t \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.835 \\ -0.293 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Hidden State Output**

For dimension 1: $h_t^{(1)} = 0.731 \times \tanh(0.835) = 0.731 \times 0.683 = 0.499$

For dimension 2: $h_t^{(2)} = 0.574 \times \tanh(-0.293) = 0.574 \times (-0.285) = -0.164$

$$h_t \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.499 \\ -0.164 \end{bmatrix}$$

This compact numerical trace illustrates exactly how the gates modulate the cell and hidden state — a typical 7-mark derivation-style question KTU examiners love.

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Gating Analogy Map)

To reinforce the conceptual understanding, the following architecture flow maps each gate to its **functional engineering analogy**, which is what the KTU theory paper expects in 7-mark conceptual questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Raw Inputs h_t-1 and x_t"] --> B["Forget Gate f_t: Decide WHAT TO ERASE from past memory"]
    A --> C["Input Gate i_t: Decide WHAT NEW INFO is worth storing"]
    A --> D["Candidate Cell C_tilde_t: CREATE new candidate values via tanh"]
    A --> E["Output Gate o_t: Decide WHAT TO EMIT as output"]

    B --> F["Multiplicative Filter on C_t-1: Erase irrelevant history"]
    C --> G["Multiplicative Filter on C_tilde_t: Select new values"]
    D --> G
    F --> H["Additive Merge: New Cell State C_t"]
    G --> H
    H --> I["Tanh Squashing of C_t: Prepare values"]
    E --> J["Multiplicative Filter on tanh C_t: Output Selection"]
    I --> J
    J --> K["Final Hidden State h_t: Passed to next time-step"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** When asked *"Draw the LSTM architecture"*, always label:
> 1. All **four input arrows** entering each gate.
> 2. The **two multiplication operations** $\odot$ (forget × old cell, input × candidate).
> 3. The **one addition operation** $+$ producing the new cell state.
> 4. The **self-loop** on $C_t$ showing its persistence across time.
