# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Contractive Autoencoder — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input x in R^d] --> B[Encoder Linear: Wx + b]
    B --> C[Activation: sigmoid or tanh]
    C --> D[Latent h in R^p]
    D --> E[Decoder Linear: W prime h + b prime]
    E --> F[Reconstruction x hat]
    F --> G[MSE Loss: x vs x hat]
    C --> H[Compute Jacobian J = dh slash dx]
    H --> I[Frobenius Norm Squared]
    I --> J[Penalty: lambda times norm J squared]
    G --> K[Total Loss L recon + lambda norm J squared]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Backprop + Weight Update]
```

### Architectural Interpretation

| Component | Purpose |
|:----------|:--------|
| **Input $x$** | Raw data (image, sensor reading, etc.) |
| **Encoder Linear** | Affine transformation producing pre-activation |
| **Activation** | Non-linearity (sigmoid/tanh); differentiator for Jacobian |
| **Latent $h$** | Compressed, smooth representation (the "map") |
| **Decoder** | Reconstructs input from latent code |
| **MSE Loss** | Ensures information preservation |
| **Jacobian Branch** | Side path — measures sensitivity of $h$ to $x$ |
| **Frobenius Penalty** | Enforces local smoothness of the encoder |
| **Total Loss** | Combined objective backpropagated through both paths |

---

## 4.2 GAN — Adversarial Training Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Generator
        Z[Noise z in R^n] --> GenG[G: Neural Network]
        GenG --> FakeImg[Generated Sample G of z]
    end

    subgraph Discriminator
        RealImg[Real Sample x in p data] --> Concat[Input Layer]
        FakeImg --> Concat
        Concat --> DiscD[D: Neural Network]
        DiscD --> Prob[Probability Real: 0 to 1]
    end

    Prob --> LossD[Discriminator Loss: log D of x + log 1 minus D of G of z]
    LossD --> UpdateD[Update D weights via gradient ascent]
    UpdateD --> DiscD

    Prob --> LossG[Generator Loss: log 1 minus D of G of z]
    LossG --> UpdateG[Update G weights via gradient descent]
    UpdateG --> GenG
```

### Training Dynamics — Equilibrium State

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Initial: D easily distinguishes real and fake] --> B[D becomes more accurate]
    B --> C[G improves to fool D]
    C --> D[D improves again]
    D --> E[G catches up]
    E --> F[Nash Equilibrium: D of x equals 0.5 for all x]
    F --> G[p g equals p data: generated distribution matches real]
```

---

## 4.3 GAN Variants — Architectural Family Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    GAN[Vanilla GAN 2014] --> DCGAN[DCGAN: Strided Conv 2015]
    GAN --> cGAN[Conditional GAN 2014]
    GAN --> InfoGAN[InfoGAN: Mutual Info 2016]
    GAN --> WGAN[Wasserstein GAN 2017]
    WGAN --> WGANGP[WGAN-GP: Gradient Penalty]
    GAN --> LSGAN[Least Squares GAN 2017]
    GAN --> CycleGAN[CycleGAN: Unpaired Translation 2017]
    GAN --> ProGAN[Progressive GAN 2017]
    ProGAN --> StyleGAN[StyleGAN: AdaIN 2019]
    StyleGAN --> StyleGAN2[StyleGAN2: Improved 2020]
```

### Key Variant Specifications

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ConditionalGAN
        Y[Condition y: label or text] --> Gcond[G of z and y]
        Y --> Dcond[D of x and y]
    end
    subgraph CycleGAN
        X[X domain image] --> Gxy[G of x]
        Gxy --> Y2[Y domain image]
        Y2 --> Fyx[F of y]
        Fyx --> X2[Reconstructed X]
    end
    subgraph InfoGAN
        Z2[Noise z] --> Ginfo[G of z and c]
        C2[Latent code c] --> Ginfo
        Ginfo --> Out[Output]
        Out --> Q[Q network estimates c prime]
    end
```

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Bank]**
**What is a Contractive Autoencoder? Explain the role of the Jacobian penalty term in its objective function.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Contractive Autoencoder (CAE) is a regularized autoencoder that adds a penalty term to the reconstruction loss to encourage locally invariant representations. The objective function is:
$$\mathcal{L}_{CAE} = L(x, \hat{x}) + \lambda \cdot \|J_f(x)\|_F^2$$
where $J_f(x) = \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}$ is the Jacobian of the encoder mapping. The Frobenius norm penalty forces the encoder to learn features that are **insensitive to small perturbations** of the input, effectively contracting the input space locally around each training point. The hyperparameter $\lambda$ controls the trade-off between reconstruction fidelity and contraction strength. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Bank]**
**Explain the adversarial training process in a GAN. What is the role of the generator and discriminator?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) consists of two networks trained in opposition:
1. **Generator $G$**: Takes random noise $z \sim p_z(z)$ as input and produces synthetic samples $G(z)$ that mimic real data.
2. **Discriminator $D$**: Receives both real samples $x \sim p_{data}$ and fake samples $G(z)$, and outputs a probability $D(\cdot)$ indicating whether the input is real.

The objective is a min-max game:
$$\min_G \max_D V(D,G) = \mathbb{E}_x[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_z[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$$
$D$ tries to correctly classify real vs. fake (maximizes $V$), while $G$ tries to fool $D$ (minimizes $V$). At equilibrium, $D(x) = 0.5$ and the generator distribution matches the real data distribution. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### **Part (a)** **[KTU University Exam – Module 4, Expected 2024/25]**
**Derive the optimal discriminator $D^*(x)$ for a GAN with a fixed generator. Show that at the Nash equilibrium, $D^*(x) = 0.5$ everywhere.** **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: State the value function.** (1 mark)
$$V(D, G) = \int_x p_{data}(x)\log D(x)\, dx + \int_z p_z(z)\log(1 - D(G(z)))\, dz$$

**Step 2: Change variable in the second integral.** (1 mark)
With $x = G(z)$, the second integral becomes:
$$V(D, G) = \int_x \left[p_{data}(x)\log D(x) + p_g(x)\log(1 - D(x))\right] dx$$

**Step 3: Differentiate the integrand with respect to $D(x)$.** (1 mark)
$$\frac{\partial}{\partial D(x)}\left[p_{data}(x)\log D(x) + p_g(x)\log(1-D(x))\right] = \frac{p_{data}(x)}{D(x)} - \frac{p_g(x)}{1-D(x)} = 0$$

**Step 4: Solve for $D^*(x)$.** (2 marks)
$$p_{data}(x)(1 - D^*(x)) = p_g(x) D^*(x)$$
$$D^*(x) = \frac{p_{data}(x)}{p_{data}(x) + p_g(x)}$$

**Step 5: Nash equilibrium condition.** (2 marks)
At equilibrium $p_g(x) = p_{data}(x)$, so:
$$D^*(x) = \frac{p_{data}(x)}{2 \cdot p_{data}(x)} = \frac{1}{2}$$
This holds for all $x$, confirming that the discriminator is maximally confused at equilibrium.

---

#### **Part (b)** **[KTU University Exam – Module 4, Expected 2024/25]**
**Explain the Wasserstein GAN (WGAN). Derive its loss function and explain how the Lipschitz constraint is enforced.** **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Define Wasserstein-1 distance.** (1 mark)
$$W(p_{data}, p_g) = \inf_{\gamma \in \Pi(p_{data}, p_g)} \mathbb{E}_{(x,y) \sim \gamma}[\|x - y\|]$$

**Step 2: Apply Kantorovich-Rubinstein duality.** (2 marks)
$$W(p_{data}, p_g) = \sup_{\|f\|_L \leq 1}\left[\mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{data}}[f(x)] - \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_g}[f(x)]\right]$$
where the supremum is over all 1-Lipschitz functions $f$.

**Step 3: Parameterize $f$ as critic network $f_w$.** (1 mark)
$$L_{critic} = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_g}[f_w(x)] - \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{data}}[f_w(x)]$$
Generator loss: $\min_G L_G = -\mathbb{E}_{z}[f_w(G(z))]$

**Step 4: Enforce Lipschitz constraint via weight clipping.** (1.5 marks)
After each critic update:
$$w \leftarrow \text{clip}(w, -c, c)$$

**Step 5: Alternative — Gradient Penalty (WGAN-GP).** (1.5 marks)
$$\mathcal{L}_{GP} = \lambda_{GP}\,\mathbb{E}_{\hat{x}}\left[(\|\nabla_{\hat{x}} f_w(\hat{x})\|_2 - 1)^2\right]$$
where $\hat{x} = \epsilon x + (1-\epsilon)G(z)$, with $\epsilon \sim U[0,1]$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### **Part (a)**
**Explain the architecture and working of a Contractive Autoencoder (CAE). Derive the closed-form Jacobian penalty for a sigmoid encoder.** **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: CAE architecture.** (1.5 marks)
A CAE is an encoder-decoder network with an additional regularization term in the loss. Encoder: $h = \sigma(Wx + b)$. Decoder: $\hat{x} = \sigma'(W'h + b')$.

**Step 2: Objective function.** (1 mark)
$$\mathcal{L} = \|x - \hat{x}\|^2 + \lambda \sum_{i,j}\left(\frac{\partial h_i}{\partial x_j}\right)^2$$

**Step 3: Compute Jacobian entry.** (1.5 marks)
$$h_i = \sigma(u_i), \quad u_i = \sum_k W_{ik}x_k + b_i$$
$$\frac{\partial h_i}{\partial x_j} = \sigma'(u_i) W_{ij} = h_i(1-h_i) W_{ij}$$

**Step 4: Frobenius norm squared.** (2 marks)
$$\|J_f(x)\|_F^2 = \sum_{i,j}\left[h_i(1-h_i)\right]^2 W_{ij}^2 = \sum_i \left[h_i(1-h_i)\right]^2 \sum_j W_{ij}^2$$

**Step 5: Compact closed form.** (1 mark)
$$\boxed{\|J_f(x)\|_F^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{d_h}\left[h_i(1-h_i)\right]^2 \|W_i\|^2}$$

---

#### **Part (b)**
**Compare DCGAN, Conditional GAN, and CycleGAN in terms of architecture, objective function, and applications.** **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution (Tabular Comparison):**

| Feature | DCGAN | Conditional GAN (cGAN) | CycleGAN |
|:--------|:------|:-----------------------|:---------|
| **Architecture** | Generator/Discriminator using strided & transposed convolutions | Generator/Discriminator conditioned on label $y$ | Two generators $G: X \to Y$, $F: Y \to X$ with two discriminators |
| **Key Equation** | Same as vanilla GAN but with conv layers | $\min_G \max_D [\log D(x \mid y) + \log(1 - D(G(z \mid y) \mid y))]$ | Adds cycle consistency: $\mathcal{L}_{cyc} = \|F(G(x)) - x\| + \|G(F(y)) - y\|$ |
| **Conditioning** | Unconditional | Label/text/class $y$ is fed to both $G$ and $D$ | Unpaired domain translation (no labels) |
| **Training Data** | Unlabeled images | Labeled image-class pairs | Unpaired image sets from two domains |
| **Application** | Image generation, super-resolution | Class-conditional synthesis, text-to-image | Style transfer, season translation, SAR→optical |
| **Limitation** | No control over output class | Requires paired labeled data | No fine-grained control over attributes |

**(7 marks distributed: 2 marks for DCGAN, 2.5 marks for cGAN derivation, 2.5 marks for CycleGAN cycle loss)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes in This Module:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing the regularization:** Students frequently apply the Jacobian penalty to **both encoder and decoder**. Remember — only the **encoder** is penalized in CAE. *(Lose 1-2 marks)*
> 
> 2. **Confusing $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$ minimization:** Early in training, when $D$ easily rejects fakes, $D(G(z)) \approx 0$, making $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$ saturate (gradient $\to 0$). Goodfellow's practical recommendation: minimize $-\log D(G(z))$ instead during early training. *(Lose 1 mark if not mentioned)*
> 
> 3. **Mixing up JSD and W-distance:** When asked about WGAN's advantage over vanilla GAN, students often mention "more stable training" but **fail to state the precise reason** — i.e., JSD vanishes for disjoint distributions while W-distance provides non-zero, continuous gradients everywhere. *(Lose 2 marks)*
> 
> 4. **Missing the $-log 4$ term** in the GAN optimal value derivation. The full expression is $V^* = -\log 4 + 2 \cdot \text{JSD}(p_{data} \| p_g)$. *(Lose 1 mark)*
> 
> 5. **Not stating the Lipschitz constraint** when explaining WGAN — the weight clipping or gradient penalty is the central mechanism, not just "using Wasserstein distance." *(Lose 2 marks)*
> 
> 6. **CycleGAN direction confusion:** Students sometimes describe it as "image-to-image translation using paired data." CycleGAN is for **unpaired** data — this is its defining feature. *(Lose 2 marks)*

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📋 **Rapid Revision Checklist — Contractive Encoders, GAN & Variants**

### 🎯 Contractive Autoencoders
- ✅ CAE penalizes the **Frobenius norm squared of the encoder Jacobian**: $\|J_f(x)\|_F^2$.
- ✅ Objective: $\mathcal{L} = L_{recon} + \lambda \|J_f(x)\|_F^2$.
- ✅ For sigmoid encoder: $\|J_f(x)\|_F^2 = \sum_i [h_i(1-h_i)]^2 \|W_i\|^2$.
- ✅ Enforces **local contraction**: $\|f(x) - f(x')\| \leq \gamma \|x - x'\|$.
- ✅ Applications: manifold learning, denoising, anomaly detection.
- ✅ Difference from DAE: DAE uses stochastic corruption; CAE uses analytic penalty.

### 🎯 Vanilla GAN
- ✅ Value function: $\min_G \max_D V(D,G) = \mathbb{E}_x[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_z[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$.
- ✅ Optimal discriminator: $D^*(x) = \frac{p_{data}(x)}{p_{data}(x) + p_g(x)}$.
- ✅ At equilibrium: $D^* = 0.5$ everywhere, $V^* = -\log 4$.
- ✅ Issues: mode collapse, vanishing gradients, training instability.
- ✅ Practical trick: minimize $-\log D(G(z))$ instead of $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$.

### 🎯 DCGAN
- ✅ Replaces MLP with **strided convolutions** (discriminator) and **transposed convolutions** (generator).
- ✅ Uses batch normalization in both networks.
- ✅ Uses LeakyReLU in discriminator, ReLU in generator.

### 🎯 Conditional GAN (cGAN)
- ✅ Conditions both $G$ and $D$ on auxiliary info $y$ (label, text).
- ✅ Objective: $\min_G \max_D [\log D(x|y) + \log(1 - D(G(z|y)|y))]$.

### 🎯 InfoGAN
- ✅ Maximizes **mutual information** between latent code $c$ and generated output.
- ✅ Adds a Q-network to recover $c$ from $G(z,c)$.
- ✅ Learns **disentangled** representations without supervision.

### 🎯 Wasserstein GAN (WGAN)
- ✅ Uses **Wasserstein-1 (Earth Mover's)** distance instead of JSD.
- ✅ Provides smooth gradients even when distributions are disjoint.
- ✅ Critic (not discriminator) enforces 1-Lipschitz via **weight clipping** or **gradient penalty**.
- ✅ Loss correlates with sample quality — useful as a training monitor.

### 🎯 LSGAN, CycleGAN, StyleGAN
- ✅ **LSGAN**: Replaces sigmoid cross-entropy with **least-squares loss** — avoids vanishing gradient when $D$ confidently rejects fakes.
- ✅ **CycleGAN**: Two generators $G, F$ with **cycle consistency loss** $\mathcal{L}_{cyc}$; no paired data needed.
- ✅ **StyleGAN**: Uses **adaptive instance normalization (AdaIN)** to inject style at each resolution; produces high-fidelity face images.

> 🎯 **High-Yield Mnemonic for Exams:**
> *"**C**ontractive **C**ompresses **J**acobian"* for CAE.
> *"**G**oodfellow's **G**ame: **G**enerator fakes, **D**iscriminator detects"* for GAN.
> *"**W**asserstein **W**anders smoothly where **JSD jumps"* for WGAN's advantage.
